Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1, draw the position of the eye of the student when measuring h0.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: An eye drawn level with the end of the rule, looking towards the rule, such that the dotted line (extended) passes through the representation of the eye. Not accepted: Eye not aligned properly.

Variant_1.question_text: Complete Fig. 1.3 to show the values of d.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: The values of d are 0.5 cm, 1.3 cm, 2.1 cm, 2.8 cm, 3.5 cm, and 4.3 cm, all correct as calculated from h0 minus h for each m. Not accepted: Any incorrect values.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.4, plot the graph of d / cm on the y-axis against m / g on the x-axis. Start your graph from the origin and draw the straight line of best fit.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 4 marks as follows: Part A: Axes - 1 mark for correctly labelled axes. Part B: Scales - 1 mark for appropriate scales (e.g., x: 2 cm ≡ 20 g, y: 2 cm ≡ 0.5 cm). Part C: Plotting points - 1 mark for accurately plotting all points. Part D: Best fit straight line - 1 mark for drawing a straight line that best fits the points, not necessarily through the origin. Not accepted: Incorrect scales or misplotted points.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain how the graph shows that d is not directly proportional to m.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: The line does not pass through the origin, indicating that d is not directly proportional to m. Not accepted: Any explanation that contradicts the graph evidence.

Variant_1.question_text: Find the gradient of your graph. Show your working clearly.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks as follows: Part A: Calculation - 1 mark for using at least half the grid for the triangle (e.g., values seen in working). Part B: Answer - 1 mark for gradient = 0.038 (with units cm/g, though other units may be used, but not 0.04). Not accepted: Incorrect calculations or units.

Variant_1.question_text: Use information from (b) to find the length L along the clamped metre rule from the edge of the bench to the point where the hanging mass is attached.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: L = 0.85 m or 85 cm. Unit required. Not accepted: Values without units or incorrect calculations.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the value of E.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: E = 11.6 (error carried forward from previous parts; no unit needed). Not accepted: Incorrect values based on prior errors.

Variant_1.question_text: Find the average time tav for the wave to travel 5.0 m along the spring.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: tav = 1.71 seconds. Not accepted: Incorrect averaging or values.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the average speed vav of the wave pulse along the spring. Give your answer to a suitable number of significant figures.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks as follows: Part A: Calculation - 1 mark for 2.924 m/s (error carried forward from (a)(i)). Part B: Answer - 1 mark for 2.9 m/s or 2.92 m/s with unit required. Not accepted: Incorrect significant figures or missing units.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why, in practice, the position of the students and the metre rules causes a parallax error.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: The student is not in line with the end of the rule, or there is a distance between the rule and the spring, or between the spring and the students. Not accepted: Vague explanations without reference to alignment.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why this parallax error causes the measured values of t to be too small.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: The stopwatch is started after the wave has passed the start or stopped before the wave reaches the end, or the observed distance is smaller than 5 m. Not accepted: Incorrect reasons unrelated to timing.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain one other reason why the times recorded by the students are not all exactly the same.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: Students have different reaction times or are in different positions. Not accepted: Reasons already covered in previous parts.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the students could measure the time t more accurately.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks as follows: Part A: Starting accurately - 1 mark for methods like the teacher or a student saying 'go' as the wave starts, and the student standing at the start of the spring or rules. Part B: Stopping accurately - 1 mark for methods like a student at the end saying 'stop'. Not accepted: Methods that do not improve accuracy.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest a method of making a wave pulse travel more slowly along the spring.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: Immerse the spring in a fluid like water or oil, decrease the tension in the spring, or make the spring shorter by having the teacher closer to the student. Not accepted: Methods that do not affect speed.

Variant_1.question_text: In the space below, draw the circuit diagram of a circuit used to measure the resistance of the thermistor.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks as follows: Part A: Circuit - 1 mark for a circuit containing the thermistor and a power supply, or an ohmmeter with the thermistor. Part B: Components - 1 mark for an ammeter in series and a voltmeter in parallel with the thermistor, or correct ohmmeter symbol with no other components. Not accepted: Incomplete circuits or incorrect placements.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain, with the aid of a diagram, how the temperature of the thermistor is changed and measured.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 3 marks as follows: Part A: Method - 1 mark for using a thermometer and water or oil bath. Part B: Temperature change - 1 mark for heating the water bath or describing how temperature is changed. Part C: Accuracy - 1 mark for placing the thermometer close to the thermistor, stirring the water, or allowing it to settle. Not accepted: Methods without diagrams or unclear explanations.

Variant_1.question_text: The variation of the resistance of a thermistor with temperature is investigated. Explain why readings are taken at different temperatures.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: To show that the relationship may not be linear, or to plot a graph and get a good line of best fit for more accurate results. Not accepted: Vague statements without reference to the investigation.

Variant_1.question_text: State how the force is produced and how it is kept the same for both pencils.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks as follows: Part A: Force production - 1 mark for explaining how force is produced, e.g., balancing weight or mass on top of the pencil or dropping it. Part B: Force consistency - 1 mark for ensuring the same weight is used on both pencils or dropped from the same height. Not accepted: Inconsistent methods.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe the shape and depth of the indentations in the plasticine.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Acceptable answers: The indentations are correctly shaped and the one for the pointed pencil is deeper. Not accepted: Descriptions without comparison.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how the student checks that A is horizontal. You may draw on Fig. 1.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Use of a spirit level, or a plumb line with a set-square, or checking that the height at two points on the rule (at least 50 cm apart) is the same distance above the bench.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how the student checks that the two strings are equal distances from the centre of metre rule B.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Checking the strings at 50 cm plus or minus d from the centre, or measuring both distances d from the centre of metre rule B, or measuring from the ends of rule B.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 1.2, mark where the eye of the student is placed to observe the rotations.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Marking the eye on Fig. 1.2 either at eye level with rule B looking towards B, or above rule A looking down close to the end of rule A; OR on Fig. 1.3, marking the eye looking toward fixed rule A from the end not B, or on top of rule A close to the end.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how the student determines T accurately.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 3 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for timing several oscillations (between 5 and 40) and dividing by the number of oscillations to find T. Award 1 mark for repeating the timing and taking an average. Award 1 mark for using a fiducial marker, timing from the centre or where speed is maximum, or ensuring smooth swings with the same amplitude.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 1.5, plot the graph of T / s on the y-axis against d / cm on the x-axis. Start your graph from d = 10.0 cm and T = 1.00 s. Draw the curved line of best fit.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 4 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for axes labelled with both quantity and unit, with T on the y-axis. Award 1 mark for scales that use at least half the grid in both directions and are sensible, starting at (10,1) with x: 2 cm ≡ 5 cm and y: 2 cm ≡ 0.2 or 0.25 s. Award 1 mark for accurate plotting to within ± half a small square with dots no larger than 1 mm. Award 1 mark for a reasonable attempt at a smooth curve.

Variant_2.question_text: Use data from the table to show that T is inversely proportional to d.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 2 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for attempting to double and halve values or showing T × d. Award 1 mark for numerical support using data, such as correct values of T × d or showing T ∝ 1/d with two values.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a reason why values for d smaller than 15 cm are not measured.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: It takes a long time to take readings, or the swings are unstable, or it is difficult to oscillate or rotate.

Variant_2.question_text: The student suggests using the second hand of a wall clock to measure the time. Another student suggests using a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s. Suggest and explain which timer is the more suitable to use.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The wall clock is more suitable because it only needs to measure to the nearest second and is accurate enough for the time measured, or the stopwatch is more suitable because it is easier to hold or closer to the apparatus.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 2.2, complete the headings of the table.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 2 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for headings of quantities time or t and temperature or θ or T with appropriate units (minutes or min for time, °C for temperature). Award 1 mark for correct units: minutes or min and °C.

Variant_2.question_text: The student uses the data to plot a graph of the variation with time of the temperature of the water. On the axes of Fig. 2.3, sketch the shape of the graph obtained. Mark any known values on the graph.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 3 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for a correct shape of the curve (two straight lines joined by a small curve, not just two straight lines). Award 1 mark for the line starting from t = 0 and θ above 0 (room temperature). Award 1 mark for a horizontal line from approximately t = 20 min at θ = 60°C, with 20 min and 60°C labelled.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the temperature of the water does not rise above 60°C.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The heat gained from the heater equals the heat lost to the surroundings, reaching equilibrium, or the heater is not powerful enough.

Variant_2.question_text: Record the reading from the newton meter to find the weight W20 of the 20 coins.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The reading is 1.5 N.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure accurately the diameter d of the coin.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The diameter d is 2.6 cm ± 0.05 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: The average thickness t of a coin is 0.19 cm. Describe how the student could verify this.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 2 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for measuring the thickness of N coins and dividing by N, or repeating the measurement. Award 1 mark for using a vernier caliper or micrometer screw gauge, or using a rule to measure a pile of at least 10 coins and dividing by the number.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the density of the coin material.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The density is 7.4 g/cm³ (or ecf from previous parts).

Variant_2.question_text: State whether the coin is made of pure copper and justify your answer.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: No, because the density is different from pure copper; or Yes, because the densities are similar if the calculated density is between 8.0 and 10.0 g/cm³; or Not sure, with a suitable comment such as the densities are close but there are uncertainties in the experiment.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe an experiment to compare the strength of two magnets.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 3 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for a diagram of the apparatus that would work (not if major error, e.g., paperclips hanging from middle of magnet). Award 1 mark for describing how the apparatus is used, such as a point of procedure or ensuring a fair test. Award 1 mark for explaining how it shows which is stronger.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how to distinguish between a magnet and a piece of soft iron using a plotting compass.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Using repulsion to identify magnets or using attraction to identify soft iron.

Variant_3.question_text: Describe how the student checks that A is horizontal. You may draw on Fig. 1.1.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing a method to check if metre rule A is horizontal, such as using a spirit level placed on the rule or ensuring that a small object placed on the rule does not roll off. Acceptable answers include: placing a spirit level on metre rule A and checking if the bubble is centred, or hanging a plumb line from the edge and verifying alignment.

Variant_3.question_text: Describe how the student checks that the two strings are equal distances from the centre of metre rule B.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing a method to verify that the strings are equidistant from the centre of metre rule B, such as measuring the distance from the centre to each string attachment point using a ruler and ensuring both distances are the same. Acceptable answers include: using a ruler to measure from the midpoint of metre rule B to each string and confirming equality.

Variant_3.question_text: On Fig. 1.2, mark where the eye of the student is placed to observe the rotations.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly marking the position on Fig. 1.2 where the student's eye should be, such as directly in line with the plane of rotation of metre rule B to ensure accurate observation. The marking should indicate a point level with and aligned to the centre of metre rule B.

Variant_3.question_text: Describe how the student determines T accurately.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award up to 3 marks for describing an accurate method to determine T, the time for one complete rotation of metre rule B. Award 1 mark for starting the timer when metre rule B passes a reference point in one direction; award 1 mark for stopping the timer after it completes one full cycle (forward and back); award 1 mark for using a stopwatch to measure to at least 0.1 second accuracy and repeating for an average. Acceptable answers include: using a stopwatch to time from the starting position through one full oscillation and back, ensuring the student views from the correct angle as marked in (c)(i).

Variant_3.question_text: On Fig. 1.5, plot the graph of T / s on the y-axis against d / cm on the x-axis. Start your graph from d = 10.0 cm and T = 1.00 s. Draw the curved line of best fit.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award up to 4 marks for the graph: 1 mark for correct scaling and labelling of axes; 1 mark for plotting at least five points accurately; 1 mark for drawing a smooth curved line of best fit; 1 mark for ensuring the graph starts from d = 10.0 cm and T = 1.00 s as specified. Not accepted: straight lines or incorrect scales.

Variant_3.question_text: Use data from the table to show that T is inversely proportional to d.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for demonstrating inverse proportionality: 1 mark for calculating 1/T for each d and showing it is proportional to d (e.g., by plotting or calculating a constant product); 1 mark for using specific values from Fig. 1.4, such as showing d × T is approximately constant (e.g., 15.0 cm × 2.74 s ≈ 41.1, and verifying for other pairs). Acceptable answers include: evidence from calculations like d1 × T1 = d2 × T2 within experimental error.

Variant_3.question_text: Suggest a reason why values for d smaller than 15 cm are not measured.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting a valid reason, such as the metre rule B becoming unstable or the strings interfering with the clamp at smaller distances, which could affect the accuracy of measurements. Acceptable answers include: safety concerns or the apparatus not functioning properly below 15 cm.

Variant_3.question_text: The student suggests using the second hand of a wall clock to measure the time. Another student suggests using a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s. Suggest and explain which timer is the more suitable to use.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting the stopwatch and explaining why it is more suitable, such as it provides greater precision (reading to 0.01 s) for timing over minutes, whereas the second hand of a wall clock may lack accuracy due to potential movement or external factors. Acceptable answers include: the stopwatch is more precise for long durations like 30 minutes.

Variant_3.question_text: The student draws a table to record the results. On Fig. 2.2, complete the headings of the table.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for completing the table headings: 1 mark for correctly labelling the time column (e.g., 'Time / min'); 1 mark for correctly labelling the temperature column (e.g., 'Temperature / °C'). Ensure headings include units as appropriate.

Variant_3.question_text: The student uses the data to plot a graph of the variation with time of the temperature of the water. On the axes of Fig. 2.3, sketch the shape of the graph obtained. Mark any known values on the graph.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award up to 3 marks for the sketch: 1 mark for showing an initial rise in temperature; 1 mark for indicating it levels off at 60 °C after 20 minutes; 1 mark for marking known values, such as starting at room temperature (e.g., around 20 °C at time 0). The graph should show a curve rising to 60 °C and then remaining constant.

Variant_3.question_text: Explain why the temperature of the water does not rise above 60°C.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for explaining that the temperature stabilizes at 60 °C because the water is likely boiling or the heater is not providing enough energy to raise it further, but based on context, it may be due to the water reaching its boiling point at that pressure or the heater maintaining equilibrium. Acceptable answers include: the heat energy is used to maintain the temperature rather than increase it, possibly due to evaporation or steady state.

Variant_3.question_text: Record the reading from the newton meter to find the weight W20 of the 20 coins. W20 =
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording the reading accurately from the newton meter diagram, such as 'W20 = 2.5 N' if that is the indicated value, ensuring the student reads the scale correctly. Not accepted: estimates without reference to the diagram.

Variant_3.question_text: Measure accurately the diameter d of the coin. d =
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring the diameter from Fig. 3.2 accurately, such as 'd = 2.5 cm' based on the diagram, using a ruler on the full-size diagram provided. Ensure the measurement is to the nearest millimetre or as per standard practice.

Variant_3.question_text: The average thickness t of a coin is 0.19 cm. State the volume V of one coin.
Variant_3.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the volume using the formula for a cylinder, V = π r² h, where r is half of d from (b) and h is t (0.19 cm). For example, if d = 2.5 cm, then r = 1.25 cm, so V = π (1.25)² (0.19). Ensure the answer is in cm³ and rounded appropriately.

Variant_1.question_text: A plumber measures, as accurately as possible, the length and internal diameter of a straight copper pipe. The length is approximately 80 cm and the internal diameter is approximately 2 cm. What is the best combination of instruments for the plumber to use? internal diameter: A rule, B rule, C vernier calipers, D vernier calipers; length: A rule, B tape, C rule, D tape
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: vernier calipers for internal diameter and rule for length.

Variant_1.question_text: What is the correct unit for the quantity shown? quantity: A electromotive force (e.m.f.) N, B latent heat J, C pressure kg / m3, D weight kg
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: latent heat with unit J.

Variant_1.question_text: A skydiver falls from rest through the air and reaches terminal velocity. What is the acceleration of the skydiver during his fall? A constant at 0 m / s2, B constant at 10 m / s2, C starting at 0 m / s2 and increasing to 10 m / s2, D starting at 10 m / s2 and decreasing to 0 m / s2
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: starting at 10 m / s2 and decreasing to 0 m / s2.

Variant_1.question_text: A car accelerates from traffic lights for 10 s. It stays at a steady speed for 20 s and then brakes to a stop in 3 s. Which graph shows the journey? A speed increasing then constant then decreasing, B speed increasing then constant then decreasing with specific time scales, C and D other graph options
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: the graph showing speed increasing over 10 s, remaining constant for 20 s, then decreasing over 3 s.

Variant_1.question_text: Which vehicle has an acceleration of 5 m / s2? A a bicycle, when its speed changes from rest to 2.5 m / s in 2 s, B a car, when its speed changes from rest to 15 m / s in 5 s, C a lorry, when its speed changes from rest to 20 m / s in 15 s, D a motorbike, when its speed changes from rest to 50 m / s in 10 s
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: a motorbike, when its speed changes from rest to 50 m / s in 10 s.

Variant_1.question_text: A car moves in a circle at constant speed. What is the direction of the resultant force acting on the car? A, B, C, D with diagrams indicating directions
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: towards the centre of the circle.

Variant_1.question_text: A student collects stones and finds their density. Which apparatus is needed to measure the mass and the volume of the stones? mass: A newton meter, B newton meter, C top-pan balance, D top-pan balance; volume: A measuring cylinder and water, B ruler and calipers, C measuring cylinder and water, D ruler and calipers
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: top-pan balance for mass and measuring cylinder and water for volume.

Variant_1.question_text: A uniform beam is balanced at its midpoint. An object is placed on the beam, as shown. 30 cm 60 N. Which force will rebalance the beam? A 30 N acting upwards, 60 cm to the left of the midpoint, B 30 N acting upwards, 60 cm to the right of the midpoint, C 45 N acting downwards, 45 cm to the right of the midpoint, D 90 N acting downwards, 20 cm to the left of the midpoint
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: 30 N acting upwards, 60 cm to the left of the midpoint.

Variant_1.question_text: A student finds the centre of mass of a triangular lamina PQR. He drills a small hole at Q. He suspends the lamina from a pin through the hole at Q so that the lamina swings freely. He then hangs a plumb-line from the pin at Q, as shown. He marks the position of the plumb-line on the lamina. To determine the location of the centre of mass, the student then repeats the experiment but with one change. What is the change? A He suspends the lamina from the hole at Q, with R on the left and P on the right, B He suspends the lamina from a pin through a hole at R, C He uses a heavier weight on the plumb-line, D He uses a longer plumb-line
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: He suspends the lamina from a pin through a hole at R.

Variant_1.question_text: The same downward force is applied to four objects resting on a horizontal surface. Which exerts the greatest pressure on the surface? A stiletto heel, B table fork, C drawing pin, D car wheel
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: table fork.

Variant_1.question_text: Four identical measuring cylinders contain liquid. Two contain water of density 1000 kg / m3. Two contain paraffin of density 800 kg / m3. Which cylinder has the least pressure exerted on its base by the liquid it contains? A 80 cm3 water, B 80 cm3 paraffin, C 60 cm3 water, D 60 cm3 paraffin
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: the cylinder with 80 cm3 of paraffin.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram represents parts of a power station. coal-fired boiler turbine electricity generator. What is the order of the energy changes taking place? A chemical → heat → kinetic → electrical, B chemical → kinetic → heat → electrical, C heat → chemical → kinetic → electrical, D kinetic → chemical → heat → electrical
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: chemical → heat → kinetic → electrical.

Variant_1.question_text: The centre of the Sun produces large amounts of energy. What is the source of this energy? A chemical reaction, B nuclear fission, C nuclear fusion, D radioactive decay
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: nuclear fusion.

Variant_1.question_text: A crane lifts a weight of 1000 N through a vertical height of 30 m. It uses 60 000 J of energy. What is the efficiency of the crane? A 20 %, B 30 %, C 50 %, D 200 %
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: 50 %.

Variant_1.question_text: According to the kinetic theory, matter is made up of very small particles in a constant state of motion. Which row best describes the particle behaviour in the liquid state? forces between particles: A strong, B strong, C strong, D weak; motion of particles: A move randomly at high speed, B vibrate but are free to move position, C vibrate to and fro around a fixed position, D move randomly at high speed
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: strong forces between particles and particles vibrate but are free to move position.

Variant_1.question_text: A balloon filled with air is gently heated. What happens to the mass and the density of the air inside the balloon? mass: A decreases, B decreases, C stays the same, D stays the same; density: A decreases, B stays the same, C decreases, D stays the same
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: mass stays the same and density decreases.

Variant_1.question_text: A certain liquid is used in a liquid-in-glass thermometer. It does not expand uniformly with temperature. What effect will this have on the scale of the thermometer? A It will be non-linear, B It will have a small range, C The markings will be close together, D The markings will be far apart
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: It will be non-linear.

Variant_1.question_text: An ice pack is used to cool 0.25 kg of water. The specific heat capacity of water is 4.2 kJ / (kg °C). How much thermal energy (heat) must the ice pack extract from the water to reduce the water temperature by 15 °C? A 0.070 kJ, B 1.1 kJ, C 16 kJ, D 250 kJ
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: 16 kJ.

Variant_2.question_text: What is the correct unit for the quantity shown? quantity unit A electromotive force (e.m.f.) N B latent heat J C pressure kg / m3 D weight kg
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is latent heat with unit J.

Variant_2.question_text: A plumber measures, as accurately as possible, the length and internal diameter of a straight copper pipe. The length is approximately 80 cm and the internal diameter is approximately 2 cm. What is the best combination of instruments for the plumber to use? internal diameter length A rule rule B rule tape C vernier calipers rule D vernier calipers tape
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is vernier calipers for internal diameter and rule for length.

Variant_2.question_text: A car moves in a circle at constant speed. What is the direction of the resultant force acting on the car? A centre of circle B turned by car C D
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is towards the centre of the circle.

Variant_2.question_text: Which vehicle has an acceleration of 5 m / s2? A a bicycle, when its speed changes from rest to 2.5 m / s in 2 s B a car, when its speed changes from rest to 15 m / s in 5 s C a lorry, when its speed changes from rest to 20 m / s in 15 s D a motorbike, when its speed changes from rest to 50 m / s in 10 s
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which is a motorbike when its speed changes from rest to 50 m / s in 10 s.

Variant_2.question_text: A car accelerates from traffic lights for 10 s. It stays at a steady speed for 20 s and then brakes to a stop in 3 s. Which graph shows the journey? speed time / s 10 20 20 20 20 30 A speed time / s 10 30 B speed time / s 10 30 C speed time / s 10 30 D 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 0
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which shows the speed increasing for 10 s, remaining constant for 20 s, and then decreasing to zero in 3 s.

Variant_2.question_text: A skydiver falls from rest through the air and reaches terminal velocity. What is the acceleration of the skydiver during his fall? A constant at 0 m / s2 B constant at 10 m / s2 C starting at 0 m / s2 and increasing to 10 m / s2 D starting at 10 m / s2 and decreasing to 0 m / s2
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which is starting at 10 m / s2 and decreasing to 0 m / s2.

Variant_2.question_text: A uniform beam is balanced at its midpoint. An object is placed on the beam, as shown. 30 cm 60 N Which force will rebalance the beam? Variant_1.question_text: State the speed of the car just before the brakes are applied.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe and explain the effect of the unbalanced forces on the motion of the sky-diver.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the power input to the generator.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest a reason why only some of the kinetic energy of the water is given to the generator.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of: water after hitting turbine still moves or has kinetic energy, velocity, or energy; energy lost due to friction; friction and its location; heat or internal energy and its location; water misses turbine.

Variant_1.question_text: State what is meant by renewable energy.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: coal, oil, gas, peat, nuclear, or uranium. Do not accept solar.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the total energy removed from 1.0 kg of water as it cools from 16 °C, changes into ice, and then cools to –5 °C.

Award the second mark for: 67200 J or 10500 J or 77700 J seen, or energy equals mass times specific latent heat of fusion. Award the third mark for: 4.2 times 10^5 J.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why energy is needed to change ice into water.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: to break bonds, separate molecules, or give molecules more potential energy.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why less energy is needed to change ice at 0 °C into water at 0 °C compared to changing ice into water at a higher temperature.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of: different change in distance between molecules, molecules not so far apart, incomplete bond breaking, it doesn’t push the atmosphere back, or less work against the atmosphere.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how the student uses the spring balance to find the mass of the stone.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The student uses the spring balance to obtain a reading, which is the weight of the stone or the force of gravity on the stone. Award 1 mark for: The student divides this weight reading by the gravitational field strength, which is 10 N/kg, to calculate the mass using the formula mass equals weight divided by g.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how the student uses the measuring cylinder to find the volume of the stone.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Taking an initial reading of the measuring cylinder with liquid or water alone, such as noting the initial volume or filling to a certain level. Award 1 mark for: Measuring the increase or change in volume when the stone is totally immersed in the cylinder.

Variant_2.question_text: The mass of the stone is 150 g and its volume is 70 cm³. Calculate the density of the stone.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation of density as mass divided by volume, which is 150 g divided by 70 cm³, giving 2.1 g/cm³ or equivalent units such as 2142.86 kg/m³.

Variant_2.question_text: The stone is taken to another place, where the gravitational field strength is less than 10 N/kg. State how this affects the mass and the weight of the stone.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The mass of the stone remains unchanged. The weight of the stone decreases because the gravitational field strength is less.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe the energy changes that take place as she pedals up the hill at constant speed.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Chemical potential energy is converted at the start. Award 1 mark for: Gravitational potential energy increases. Award 1 mark for: Thermal energy, heat, or internal energy is produced.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain how the law of conservation of energy applies to the complete journey from A to B.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Energy is not created or destroyed; it only changes form, and the total energy remains constant throughout the journey. For example, energy conversions occur, such as chemical energy to kinetic and potential energy, and all energy ends up as heat.

Variant_2.question_text: At one point in the journey, the gravitational potential energy of the cyclist has increased by 5400 J. The mass of the cyclist is 60 kg. The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. Calculate the height above A of the cyclist at this point.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Using the formula for gravitational potential energy, height equals potential energy divided by mass times gravitational field strength, such as 5400 divided by 60 times 10. Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation giving 9.0 meters.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain, using ideas about molecules, why a balloon filled with gas expands when heated.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The molecules of the gas have more kinetic energy, speed, or velocity when heated. Award 1 mark for: The molecules hit the sides harder or more frequently, or with more force, initially creating a larger pressure.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain, using ideas about molecules, why a balloon filled with water expands very little when heated.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: There are larger forces between the liquid molecules or stronger bonds in the water.

Variant_2.question_text: A bubble of gas rises from the bottom of a lake to the surface. The pressure at the bottom of the lake is 3.0 × 10⁵ Pa and the pressure at the surface is 1.0 × 10⁵ Pa. The volume of the bubble at the bottom of the lake is 2.0 cm³. Calculate the volume of the bubble at the surface.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Using the formula P1 V1 equals P2 V2, such as 3.0 × 10⁵ Pa times 2.0 cm³ equals 1.0 × 10⁵ Pa times the volume at the surface. Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation giving 6.0 cm³.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: It prevents electric shock or electrocution if the case becomes live. Award 1 mark for: If the live wire touches the case, the earth wire provides a path for current to flow safely.

Variant_2.question_text: An electric heater is marked 500 W, 240 V. It is used for 45 minutes. Calculate the electrical energy used.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Converting power to 0.5 kW and time to hours, such as 45 minutes is 0.75 hours, then energy equals power times time. Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation giving 0.375 kWh or equivalent.

Variant_2.question_text: State why the cathode in a cathode-ray oscilloscope is heated.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Heating the cathode produces electrons or causes thermionic emission. Award 1 mark for: The same heating effect occurs, as it depends on the current squared.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: No beam is produced because electrons do not reach the screen or are not emitted. Award 1 mark for: The electrons or beam are repelled by the negative anode or no longer attracted by it.

Variant_2.question_text: What type of nuclear reaction occurs in a nuclear reactor?

Variant_2.question_text: What is meant by the term radioactive?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: It emits particles or ionising radiation, or there is spontaneous or random emission of radiation, or the atom or nucleus decays.

Variant_2.question_text: What is meant by the term half-life?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: It is the long time for the substance to decay or for any quantity to halve. Award 1 mark for: It is the time for halving of count, count rate, emissions, number of nuclei, atoms, or activity.

Variant_2.question_text: Substances can change from one state to another as shown. 1 melting 2 boiling 4 freezing 3 condensing solid liquid gas For substances to change from one state to another, there must be some energy transfer. Which changes involve the substance taking in energy and which changes involve the substance giving out energy? energy taken in energy given out A 1 and 2 3 and 4 B 1 and 3 2 and 4 C 2 and 4 1 and 3 D 3 and 4 1 and 2
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Option A: energy taken in for 1 (melting) and 2 (boiling), energy given out for 3 (condensing) and 4 (freezing). Award 1 mark for selecting this option.

Variant_2.question_text: How does heat transfer through a vacuum take place? A by conduction, convection and radiation B by conduction only C by convection only D by radiation only
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Option D: by radiation only. Award 1 mark for selecting this option.

Variant_2.question_text: A ray of light enters a glass block at an angle of incidence i, pr
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Option A: (as per the mark scheme key). Award 1 mark for selecting this option.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the mass of the ice cube.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the volume of the cube as 0.040 cubed equals 0.000064 cubic meters or 6.4 times 10 to the power of minus 5 cubic meters. Award 1 mark for calculating mass as density times volume, that is 920 kilograms per cubic meter times 6.4 times 10 to the power of minus 5 cubic meters. Award 1 mark for the final answer of 0.059 kilograms or 59 grams or 0.05888 kilograms.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the weight of the ice cube.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the weight as 0.59 newtons or 0.5888 newtons.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the thermal energy absorbed by the ice cube as it melts.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the energy as mass times specific latent heat of fusion, that is 0.059 kilograms times 3.4 times 10 to the power of 5 joules per kilogram. Award 1 mark for the final answer of 2.0 times 10 to the power of 4 joules or 2.01 times 10 to the power of 4 joules or 20060 joules.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why very little work is done by the electric motor when lifting up the empty elevator.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that work is done by the falling block or the block loses gravitational potential energy or energy is transferred from the block to the elevator or the forces balance. Award 1 mark for stating that the work done by the falling block raises the elevator or is converted to gravitational potential energy of the elevator or is done against friction or air resistance or is used to accelerate the elevator.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the minimum power output of the electric motor.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating work done as force times distance, that is 4900 newtons times 24 meters equals 117600 joules, or power as energy divided by time. Award 1 mark for calculating power as 4900 newtons times 24 meters divided by 28 seconds. Award 1 mark for the final answer of 4200 watts or 4.2 kilowatts.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 3.1, draw a labelled diagram of the structure of the thermocouple thermometer as it measures the temperature in the flame.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing one junction in the flame and three wires and a fixed point such as an ice bath or two wires. Award 1 mark for drawing two different metals and a voltmeter connected.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the fixed points and the reading of the voltmeter are used to determine the value of the temperature in the flame.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the voltmeter reading or voltage at fixed points, for example at 0 degrees Celsius and 100 degrees Celsius, is used. Award 1 mark for stating that the voltage in the flame is compared with the voltages at the fixed points to obtain the temperature, using a graph or equation or described in words.

Variant_1.question_text: State and explain one other advantage of using a thermocouple thermometer to determine temperature.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for any one of the following: it can measure rapidly varying temperatures because it has a small heat capacity; it allows remote measurement so the user is not near the thermometer; it provides a direct input to a computer; it has an electrical output.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 4.1, continue the three rays through the lens and into the air.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly drawing one outer ray parallel to the principal axis. Award 1 mark for correctly drawing the three rays parallel to the principal axis.

Variant_1.question_text: State what happens to the speed of light on entering the glass lens from air.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the speed is reduced or slows down.

Variant_1.question_text: State what happens to the speed of light on leaving the lens and returning to the air.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the speed returns to its original value of 3.0 times 10 to the power of 8 meters per second.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the frequency of this light.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using frequency equals speed divided by wavelength, that is 3.0 times 10 to the power of 8 meters per second divided by 6.0 times 10 to the power of minus 7 meters. Award 1 mark for the final answer of 5.0 times 10 to the power of 14 hertz.

Variant_1.question_text: State the effect, if any, on the frequency as the light enters the glass from air.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that there is no effect and the frequency remains 5.0 times 10 to the power of 14 hertz.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain how rubbing the nylon rod makes it negatively charged.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that electrons move to the rod.

Variant_1.question_text: State what happens to the cloth as the rod becomes negatively charged.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the cloth becomes positively charged or loses electrons.

Variant_1.question_text: Fig. 5.2 shows the negatively-charged rod placed near to a conducting sphere. The sphere is suspended by an insulating thread.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: For part (b)(i): Award 1 mark for showing positive charges on the right and negative charges on the left of the sphere. Award 1 mark for showing equal numbers of at least two charges that are roughly symmetrical. For part (b)(ii): Award 1 mark for stating that positive charges are attracted. Award 1 mark for stating that the attraction is larger than the repulsion or that the positives are closer to the rod than the negatives.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the mass of the cube.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating the principle of moments, that the clockwise moment equals the anticlockwise moment. Award 1 mark for setting up the equation using the moments: 0.050 kg mass at 10 cm from pivot creates a moment equal to the cube's mass at 25 cm from pivot, so 0.050 kg × 10 cm × 40 cm = cube mass × 25 cm. Award 1 mark for the correct calculation: cube mass = (0.050 × 10 × 40) / 25 = 0.080 kg or 80 g. Acceptable answers: 0.080 kg or 80 g.

Variant_2.question_text: The cube has a volume of 1.6 × 10–4 m3. Determine the density of the wood.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula for density: density = mass / volume. Award 1 mark for substituting the values: mass = 0.080 kg, volume = 1.6 × 10–4 m3, so density = 0.080 / (1.6 × 10–4) kg/m3. The correct answer is 500 kg/m3 or 0.50 g/cm3. Acceptable answers: 500 kg/m3 or 0.50 g/cm3.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the weight of the skier.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula: weight = mass × gravitational field strength. With mass = 85 kg and gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg, weight = 85 × 10 = 850 N. Acceptable answers: 850 N.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the minimum possible difference in height between T and B.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the energy conservation principle: kinetic energy at B = potential energy lost, so mgh = kinetic energy. Award 1 mark for substituting: mass = 85 kg, g = 10 N/kg, kinetic energy = 5.5 × 10^4 J, so h = (5.5 × 10^4) / (85 × 10) = 65 m. Acceptable answers: 65 m or 64.7 m approximately.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the average horizontal force that acts on the skier between B and P.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using work done equals force times distance or kinetic energy equals force times distance: force = kinetic energy / distance. Award 1 mark for substituting: kinetic energy = 5.5 × 10^4 J, distance = 33 m, so force = 5.5 × 10^4 / 33 ≈ 1667 N. Acceptable answers: 1700 N, 1670 N, 1667 N, or 1666.7 N approximately.

Variant_2.question_text: State the equation that relates the volume V2 of the helium in the balloon at launch to p1, p2 and V1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating Boyle's law: p1 V1 = p2 V2, where p1 is the initial pressure, V1 is the initial volume, p2 is the final pressure, and V2 is the final volume.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate V2.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the equation p1 V1 = p2 V2. Award 1 mark for substituting: p1 = 2.5 × 10^7 Pa, V1 = 18 m3, p2 = 1.0 × 10^5 Pa, so V2 = (p1 V1) / p2 = (2.5 × 10^7 × 18) / (1.0 × 10^5) = 4500 m3. Acceptable answers: 4500 m3.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest and explain why the balloon is only partially inflated when it leaves the ground.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting that the balloon needs to inflate further higher up. Award 1 mark for explaining that atmospheric pressure decreases with height, so if fully inflated on the ground, it would burst; or that otherwise, there would be greater upthrust, causing it to rise too high or too fast.

Variant_2.question_text: State the speed of microwaves in air.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the speed of microwaves in air is 3.00 × 10^8 m/s.

Variant_2.question_text: Determine the wavelength of the microwave shown in Fig. 4.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the wavelength as the distance between A and B divided by the number of wavelengths, which is 0.16 m or 16 cm based on the figure (not to scale, but implied from context). Acceptable answers: 0.16 m or 16 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how microwaves are used in the transmission of television signals by satellite.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 3 marks for describing: microwaves travel through space or vacuum and are not reflected by the ionosphere; they are encoded with the television signal; sent to a satellite which amplifies or boosts the signal; and then transmitted back to Earth where they are received by a satellite dish. Each point can award 1 mark, up to 3 marks total.

Variant_2.question_text: State two properties common to all electromagnetic waves.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for any two of: they travel at the same speed in a vacuum (speed of light); they can travel through a vacuum or space without a medium; they transfer energy; they are transverse waves; they consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields; they undergo reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, or polarisation.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 5.1, mark with an N the position of the north pole produced in the iron bar and mark with an S the position of the south pole produced in the iron bar.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly marking the north pole at the top end of the iron bar and the south pole at the bottom end, based on the magnetic field from the electromagnet.

Variant_2.question_text: State and explain what happens to the iron bar once it is magnetised.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the iron bar is attracted to or moves towards the iron core. Award 1 mark for explaining that unlike poles attract, as the core has a south pole near the bar's north pole.

Variant_2.question_text: State what happens to the magnetic poles of the iron bar when the switch is opened and there is no current in the wire.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the magnetic poles disappear, the bar is demagnetised, or it loses its magnetism.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The length l in the range 24.0 cm to 26.0 cm and l greater than h1 minus h2, with the correct unit seen somewhere. Award 1 mark for: All lengths recorded to the nearest millimetre or better.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Correct calculation of sin θ and θ giving a value of θ in the range 40 degrees to 80 degrees. Award 1 mark for: θ in the range 50 degrees to 70 degrees with the unit degrees.

One complete vibration is from the position of the pin, up to the highest position, down to the lowest position and back up to the position of the pin, as shown in Fig. 2.2.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Length in the range 29.6 cm to 29.8 cm and width in the range 21.0 cm to 21.2 cm, both measured to the nearest millimeter or better, with the unit included. Length and width can be interchanged.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: See the marking scheme for part (b)(i), as it includes the calculation for t.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Correct calculation of velocity v using the formula v = 2s / t with consistent units (for example, allow cm/s if s is in cm), and correct calculation of kinetic energy EK using EK = 1/2 m v^2, with the unit included. The corresponding T squared values are: 9.38 s squared, 8.95 s squared, 8.51 s squared, 8.10 s squared, 7.70 s squared, and 7.28 s squared, all to three significant figures.
