Variant_1.question_text: Pour 100 cm3 from the supply of water at room temperature into the measuring cylinder. Pour this volume of water into the beaker labelled C. Measure the temperature θ1 of the water in this beaker.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a sensible value of θ1 measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, with unit degrees Celsius. Penalise missing or wrong unit once only across parts (a) and (b).

Variant_1.question_text: Place the 100 g mass into the beaker labelled H. Ensure the string hangs over the edge of the beaker. Add hot water to beaker H up to the level of the top of the label. Place the cover over the beaker and insert the stirrer and second thermometer. Stir the water for approximately 1 minute. Measure the temperature θ2 of the water in beaker H and quickly transfer the mass from beaker H to beaker C. Try not to transfer any hot water.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for θ2 greater than 70 degrees Celsius, measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, with unit degrees Celsius.

Variant_1.question_text: Record the maximum temperature θ3 of the water in beaker C after transferring the mass.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a sensible value of θ3 measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, with unit degrees Celsius, and 2.0 degrees Celsius to 8.0 degrees Celsius higher than θ1.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the specific heat capacity of the metal from which the mass is made.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct calculation and a value for the specific heat capacity in the range 0.20 J/(g °C) to 0.60 J/(g °C). Ignore minor substitution errors. Award 1 mark for a value in the range 0.30 J/(g °C) to 0.50 J/(g °C) with unit J/(g °C).

Variant_1.question_text: Record the length L and the values of V and I for the preliminary setup.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for L recorded and in the range 9.8 cm to 10.2 cm with unit centimetres, and V in the range 0.02 V to 0.20 V. Award 1 mark for I in the range 80 mA to 220 mA, to the nearest 10 mA or better with unit milliamperes.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the resistance R.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct calculation of R with unit ohms, expecting a value between 0.2 ohms and 1.0 ohms unless there is an error carried forward from current.

Variant_1.question_text: Record a table of values for L, V, I, and R.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a table with units for L, V, I, and R. Award 1 mark for a range of L up to at least 80.0 cm. Award 1 mark for even distribution of points. Award 1 mark for four good values of V and I where V increases as L increases and I remains approximately constant. Award 1 mark for eight good values of V and I with the same conditions. For incorrect calculations of R, remove one good values mark. For systematic errors in V or I, remove one or both good values marks. Allow error carried forward if previously penalised.

Variant_1.question_text: Plot a graph of V against L.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for axes labelled with units and correct orientation. Award 1 mark for a suitable scale not based on 3, 6, 7 etc., with data occupying more than half the page in both directions. Award 1 mark for two points plotted correctly, within half a small square, only if the scale is easy to follow. Award 1 mark for best fit fine line and fine points or crosses, with line thickness no greater than the thickest lines on the grid.

Variant_1.question_text: Determine the gradient of the graph.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a triangle using more than half the drawn line. Award 1 mark for correct calculation of the gradient. Award 1 mark for a value in the range 0.040 ohms per centimetre to 0.049 ohms per centimetre to two or three significant figures for 28 swg constantan. For alternative wires, use the specified ranges.

Variant_2.question_text: Balance the metre rule on the knife-edge. The knife-edge shows the position of the centre of mass of the rule. Record the position of the knife-edge.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording the position of the centre of mass of the rule in the range 48.0 cm to 52.0 cm, measured to the nearest millimetre or 0.1 millimetre, and including the unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Place the modelling clay at one end of the metre rule and adjust the position of the 50 g mass at the other end until the rule balances. The knife-edge must remain below the centre of mass of the rule. Determine the horizontal distance x from the centre of the modelling clay to the knife-edge and the horizontal distance y from the knife-edge to the centre of the 50 g mass. x =  ...................................................... y =  ......................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for x less than 50.0 cm, measured to the nearest millimetre or 0.1 millimetre, with unit. Award 1 mark for y less than x, measured to the nearest millimetre or 0.1 millimetre, with unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain how you ensured that your measurement of y was taken to the centre of the 50 g mass.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for an explanation such as: taking readings on either side of the mass and averaging them; or using the slot in the mass to guide the location of the centre; or measuring the diameter of the mass and adding half to the reading at the left-hand side or subtracting from the reading at the right-hand side.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the mass M of the modelling clay using the relationship M = 50 y / x gram.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a correct calculation resulting in a value of 40.0 ± 3.0 grams, to two or three significant figures, with unit.

Variant_2.question_text: The time for 20 complete vibrations is t1. Take measurements to determine an accurate value of t1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a value of t1 in the range 5 seconds to 35 seconds, with unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the time T1 for one vibration.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating T1 by dividing t1 by 20, with unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Loosen the clamp and adjust the rule so that the 15.0 cm mark on the rule is at the edge of the bench. Tighten the clamp. Determine an accurate value for the time t2 taken for 20 complete vibrations with the rule in this new position.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for determining t2 and T2 correctly, with T2 less than T1, and including unit, considering it as part of the overall assessment for (b).

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the time T2 for one vibration.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: See the marking for (b)(i), as it is combined.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the value of T2 / T1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating the ratio T2 / T1. Award 1 mark for the ratio in the range 0.80 to 0.90, to two or three significant figures, without unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Pour 100 cm3 from the supply of water at room temperature into the measuring cylinder. Pour this volume of water into the beaker labelled C. Measure the temperature θ1 of the water in this beaker.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a sensible value of θ1 measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, with unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Place the 100 g mass into the beaker labelled H. Ensure the string hangs over the edge of the beaker. Add hot water to beaker H up to the level of the top of the label. Place the cover over the beaker and insert the stirrer and second thermometer as shown. Stir the water for approximately 1 minute. Measure the temperature θ2 of the water in beaker H and quickly transfer the mass from beaker H to beaker C. Try not to transfer any hot water.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for θ2 greater than 70 degrees Celsius, measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, with unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Record the maximum temperature of the water in beaker C after transferring the mass.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a sensible value of the maximum temperature, measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, with unit, and 2.0 degrees Celsius to 8.0 degrees Celsius higher than θ1.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why one end of the wire has to be clamped tightly.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following: to secure the end, to prevent slipping, to ensure the original length does not change, or to give a strong grip on the wire.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why the students watching the experiment must wear safety glasses.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: breaking wire is a hazard or it could damage the eye.

Variant_1.question_text: State what is meant by extension.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: increase in length from the original length.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why the marker is attached a long distance from the clamped end of the wire.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: a longer length increases the extension.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why some distance is left between the marker and the pulley.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: to allow for movement of the marker as the wire extends.

Variant_1.question_text: Use Fig. 1.2 to find the position of the marker. position =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: 50.3 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest why a small 1.0 N load is added before the initial reading of the position of the marker is found.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: to tension the wire, remove kinks, or make the wire straight.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why it is difficult for a student to read the position of the marker accurately.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following: the distance between the marker and the ruler, the marker edge is not vertical, or it is difficult to view from above.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe one change to the apparatus to make it easier to read the position of the marker accurately.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: use a larger marker, raise the ruler, or use a set-square as described or drawn.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.4, plot the graph of e / mm on the y-axis against F / N on the x-axis. Start your axes from the origin. Draw the line of best fit.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for axes correctly labeled with quantity and unit, and correct way round; 1 mark for scales more than half the page and sensible; 1 mark for points plotted accurately; 1 mark for best fit straight line neatly drawn.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe the relationship between F and e that is shown by your graph.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: directly proportional if the line passes within 1 square of the origin, or linear if it does not.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe what happens as the load becomes very large.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following: the wire breaks, it is no longer linear, the marker creeps, the load hits the floor, plastic extension occurs, or it is permanently deformed.

Variant_1.question_text: By taking measurements from Fig. 2.2, find the distances l1 and l2. Give your answers in metres. l1 = l2 =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: l1 = 0.022 m and l2 = 0.033 m, within ± 0.001 m.

Variant_1.question_text: The student uses the distances to calculate g, the acceleration of free fall.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for showing 9.167 or 9.17 in working, and 1 mark for the answer 9.2 m/s² based on the previous measurements.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why the value of g obtained is not accurate.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a comparison (e.g., the value is too small) plus an explanation (e.g., due to friction on the tape, air resistance, or the person holding the tape slowing it down). Accept explanations for too large values, such as faulty timer, weight swinging, or slack in the tape at the start.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 1.1, draw the thermometer in the most suitable position for measuring the temperature of the water as it cools.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing the thermometer with its bulb in the centre of the liquid.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a reason why the thermometer should be held in a clamp.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: the thermometer should be supported in the centre of the water; it should not touch the beaker; there is no need to hold it manually; or it holds the scale facing you.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how the student avoids parallax error when reading the thermometer.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: ensuring the line of sight or view or eye is level and perpendicular to the scale of the thermometer.

Variant_2.question_text: The stopclock measures to the nearest second. Suggest why, in this experiment, the student does not need to use a digital stopwatch measuring to 0.01 s.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: the experiment only requires timing every 2 minutes; the time is measured in minutes; the temperature changes slowly; the required precision is not needed; or the clock is accurate enough without 0.01 or 0.1 seconds.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the student places the stopclock close to the beaker.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: so that the student can see or read or notice the thermometer and timer together; or so that the temperature and time can be measured more accurately or quickly.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 1.3, plot the graph of θ / °C on the y-axis against t / minutes on the x-axis. Start your graph from θ = 40 °C and t = 0. Draw a curved line of best fit.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: Award 1 mark for axes correctly labeled with quantity and unit, and the correct way round, with y-axis: 2 cm equivalent to 10 °C and x-axis: 2 cm equivalent to 2 minutes. Award 1 mark for scales that cover more than half the page and are sensible. Award 1 mark for points plotted accurately. Award 1 mark for a best fit curved line neatly drawn.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the temperature of the water does not fall to 0 °C.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: the temperature cannot fall below or only falls to room temperature or the temperature of the surroundings.

Variant_2.question_text: Use your graph to determine the time taken for the temperature of the water to fall from 90 °C to 80 °C.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a time between 1.2 and 1.4 minutes, based on the graph.

Variant_2.question_text: State and explain the effect of using the wider beaker on the time taken for the temperature of the water to fall from 90 °C to 80 °C.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: the time decreases or the temperature falls or cools more quickly. Award 1 mark for: heat is lost more quickly from the larger surface area or the water evaporates more quickly from the larger surface area.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 2.1, mark and label the length l of the pendulum.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately marking and labeling the length from the support to the centre of mass of the bob.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe a method of measuring l accurately.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: stating a measuring instrument and providing additional detail on how it is used, such as adding half the measured diameter of the bob; marking the string at the correct length; measuring from the support to the top and bottom of the bob and then averaging; or using a vertical rule with a set-square described or drawn.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain how to find the average time T for one complete swing.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: explaining to add the three measurements of time for 20 complete swings and divide by 60, or using the correct equation to find the average.

Variant_2.question_text: Using the relationship g = 4π²l / T², calculate a value for g. Give your answer to 3 significant figures.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the calculation leading to 10.043 (or equivalent). Award 1 mark for the final answer of 10.0 m/s² to 3 significant figures.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest an improvement to this experiment.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: repeating the experiment for different value(s) of length and averaging the results.

Variant_1.question_text: Vernier calipers read to one tenth of a millimetre. Which reading is given to this precision? A 3.3 cm B 3.31 cm C 3.310 cm D 3.312 cm
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B (3.31 cm).

Variant_1.question_text: Velocity is given by the change in displacement divided by the change in time. How many vector quantities appear in this statement? A 0 B 1 C 2 D 3
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C (2).

Variant_1.question_text: A ball starts to roll down a steep slope and then along rough horizontal ground. Which graph best shows the speed of the ball?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D.

Variant_1.question_text: A car is stationary at a set of traffic lights. When the lights turn green, the car begins to move and continues to speed up until it reaches the maximum speed allowed. It continues to travel at this constant speed for the rest of the journey. What happens to the acceleration and to the velocity of the car during this journey? A Both the acceleration and the velocity change. B Only the acceleration changes. C Only the velocity changes. D Neither the acceleration nor the velocity changes.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A.

Variant_1.question_text: An aeroplane is half way along a runway before it takes off. The arrows show the directions of four forces acting on the aeroplane. The drag forces on the aeroplane are negligible. The gravitational field strength g is 10 N / kg. The acceleration of the aeroplane is 2 m / s2. What is the largest force? A the force on the wheels from the ground B the lift acting on the wings C the thrust from the jet engines D the weight of the aeroplane
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D.

Variant_1.question_text: The front wheel of a car is turned in a clockwise direction by the engine as the car accelerates towards the right, as shown in the diagram. There is a force of friction between the wheel and the road. What are the directions of the frictional forces on the wheel of the car and on the road? A to the left to the left B to the left to the right C to the right to the left D to the right to the right
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C.

Variant_1.question_text: Masses X and Y are placed on opposite sides of the centre of a uniform metre rule, which is pivoted at its centre. Which combination of masses and distances balances the rule? mass / g distance / cm X Y p s A 200 200 5 10 B 200 300 10 15 C 400 300 12 16 D 500 200 15 30
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C.

Variant_1.question_text: A student balances a non-uniform object on a pivot. To do this, a weight is suspended near the left-hand end of the object. Where is the centre of mass of the object?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D.

Variant_1.question_text: A spring has a length of 9 cm when loaded with a 200 g mass. The extension-mass graph for the spring is shown. The 200 g mass is replaced with a 100 g mass. What is the new length of the spring? A 7 cm B 8 cm C 9 cm D 10 cm
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B (8 cm).
Variant_1.topic_syllabus: Properties of Matter, including Hooke's Law

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows two cubes made from the same material. One cube has sides that are twice as long as the sides of the other cube. The weight of the small cube is W. What is the weight of the larger cube? A 2W B 4W C 8W D 16W
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C (8W).

Variant_1.question_text: A garden table weighs 40 N and has a top surface of area 2 m2. It is raining and the rain produces a pressure of 4 N / m2 on the table. What is the force exerted by the table on the ground? A 20 N B 32 N C 42 N D 48 N
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D (48 N).

Variant_1.question_text: Four syringes contain air at atmospheric pressure. The air in all four syringes is slowly compressed. The results are given in the table. Which syringe shows the smallest pressure change? volume before compression / cm3 volume after compression / cm3 A 50 10 B 100 50 C 400 25 D 400 100
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B.

Variant_1.question_text: Which is a non-renewable source of energy? A a battery in a watch B a hydroelectric power station C a solar panel D a wind turbine
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A.

Variant_1.question_text: A builder lifts eight slabs from the ground on to the back of a lorry 1.5 m high. The total time taken is 48 s and each slab weighs 20 N. How much useful power does the builder produce? A 5 W B 40 W C 240 W D 320 W
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A (5 W).

Variant_1.question_text: When a gas is rapidly compressed to a smaller volume, its temperature increases. What happens to the gas molecules? A They move closer together and their average speed decreases. B They move closer together and their average speed increases. C They move closer together and their average speed remains unchanged. D They stay the same distance apart and their average speed increases.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B.

Variant_1.question_text: On a hot day, the drink in a bottle can be kept cool by standing the bottle in a bowl of water and placing a wet cloth over it. Why is the drink kept cool?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D.

Variant_1.question_text: A student uses a double-walled glass vessel to contain a hot liquid. What reduces the heat loss by radiation?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D.

Variant_1.question_text: What is the reason for the constriction in a clinical thermometer? A to give the thermometer a smaller temperature range B to make the thermometer more sensitive C to prevent the mercury breaking the bulb when it expands
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D.

Variant_2.question_text: The level of water in a measuring cylinder is 75 cm3. A stone of volume 20 cm3 is lowered into the water. What is the new reading of the water level? A 20 cm3 B 55 cm3 C 75 cm3 D 95 cm3
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D: 95 cm3.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows three forces acting on a block. 3 N 2 N 11 N Which additional force will produce a resultant force of 3 N to the left? A 3 N to the left B 6 N to the right C 9 N to the left D 13 N to the right
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C: 9 N to the left.

Variant_2.question_text: A table-tennis ball is released from the top of an evacuated tube. ball vacuum Which graph shows how the speed of the ball changes with time as it falls to the bottom of the tube? A speed time 00 B speed time 00 C speed time 00 D speed time 00
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A: the graph that shows speed increasing linearly with time.

Variant_2.question_text: The speed-time graph for a car is shown. 25 20 15 10 5 0 0 10 20 30 40 50 time / s speed m / s What is the acceleration of the car at 30 s? A 0 B 30 5 25 − m / s2 C 30 25 m / s2 D 50 25 m / s2
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A: 0 m/s2.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a firework rocket. P Q R S As the rocket flies through the air, three forces act on it. These forces are weight, thrust and air resistance. What are the three forces? thrust air resistance weight A P R S B P S R C Q R S D Q S R
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D: thrust is Q, air resistance is S, weight is R.

Variant_2.question_text: An object has a mass of 15 kg. It is pushed horizontally by a force of 40 N. The frictional force is 10 N. What is the acceleration of the object? A 0.50 m / s2 B 1.5 m / s2 C 2.0 m / s2 D 2.7 m / s2
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C: 2.0 m/s2.

Variant_2.question_text: An object Y is in a fixed position on a rod. A weight X is moved and the position of a pivot is adjusted until the rod balances on the pivot, as shown. pivot Y X The experiment is repeated in a region where the gravitational field strength is lower. What is done to keep the rod balanced? pivot X A move left no movement B move right move left C no movement move right D no movement no movement
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D: no movement of the pivot and no movement of X.

Variant_2.question_text: Four solid uniform cones have equal weight. They are placed on a bench as shown in the scale diagram. Which cone is the most stable? A B C D
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C: the cone with the widest base and lowest center of gravity.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows how the length of a spring changes when a load of 10 N is hung on it. 10 N 5 cm 8 cm load The 10 N load is replaced by a 20 N load. What is the new length of the spring? A 6 cm B 11 cm C 14 cm D 16 cm
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B: 11 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: The mass and the volume of a bar made from metal X are measured. The masses and volumes of four other bars are measured. Which bar is made from a metal with a density that is double that of X? mass compared with X volume compared with X A double half B half same C same double D same half
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D: mass compared with X is the same and volume compared with X is half.

Variant_2.question_text: Four solid blocks with flat surfaces are placed on some soft plasticine, in the positions shown. Which block sinks the deepest into the plasticine? 1.0 kg 10 cm2 20 cm2 A 1.0 kg 20 cm2 10 cm2 B C 0.5 kg 10 cm2 20 cm2 0.5 kg 20 cm2 10 cm2 D
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B: the block with 1.0 kg mass and 10 cm2 base area.

Variant_2.question_text: The end of a bicycle pump is sealed. sealed end piston trapped air The temperature of the air does not change as the piston is pushed in. The final volume of trapped air is 1/4 of the original volume. How does the pressure of the trapped air change? A It decreases to 1/4 of the original value. B It decreases to 3/4 of the original value. C It increases to 3 times the original value. D It increases to 4 times the original value.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D: It increases to 4 times the original value.

Variant_2.question_text: Hydroelectric, tidal and fossil fuel power stations generate electrical energy. Do these use renewable sources of energy? hydroelectric tidal fossil fuel A no yes yes B no no yes C yes no no D yes yes no
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D: yes for hydroelectric, yes for tidal, no for fossil fuel.

Variant_2.question_text: An object of mass 5 kg is carried a distance of 4 m across a room at a constant height above the floor. What is the work done on the object? A 0 B 20 J C 50 J D 200 J
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A: 0 J.

Variant_2.question_text: A gas in a container of fixed volume is heated. What happens to the molecules of the gas? A They collide less frequently. B They expand. C They move faster. D They move further apart.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C: They move faster.

Variant_2.question_text: In hot weather, people use electric fans to keep cool. Why do the fans make them feel cool? A They change one form of energy into another. B They cool the air in the room. C They increase the rate of evaporation from the skin. D They speed up the vibration of air molecules.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C: They increase the rate of evaporation from the skin.

Variant_2.question_text: Four metal cans are identical except for the colour and the texture of their outer surfaces. 100 cm3 of water at 70 °C is poured into each can. Which cools the most rapidly? A black, rough B black, shiny C white, rough D white, shiny outer surface:
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A: black, rough.

Variant_2.question_text: A liquid-in-glass thermometer consists of a bulb containing a liquid which expands into a very thin capillary tube. bulb capillary tube The liquid in the thermometer is replaced by the same volume of a different liquid that expands more for the same temperature rise. The new thermometer will have A greater sensitivity and a greater range. B greater sensitivity but a smaller range. C the same sensitivity and the same range. D the same sensitivity but a greater range.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B: greater sensitivity but a smaller range.

Variant_2.question_text: An ice cube, at a temperature of 0 °C, has a mass of 10 g. The specific latent heat of fusion of water is 3 × 105 J / kg. How much heat energy is needed to convert the ice cube into 10 g of water at 0 °C? A 30 J B 3000 J C 3 × 104 J D 3 × 106 J
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B: 3000 J.

Variant_2.question_text: A strip is made from two metals joined together. The diagrams show the strip at room temperature and after it has been cooled. brass invar brass invar below room temperature at room temperature The change in shape occurs because A brass contracts more than invar. B brass expands when it cools
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A: brass contracts more than invar.

Variant_1.question_text: A parachutist of total weight 950 N falls vertically at a constant velocity of 6.5 m/s. State the size and direction of the resistive (drag) force acting on the parachutist.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the size of the resistive force as 950 N. Award 1 mark for the direction as upwards.

Variant_1.question_text: At a certain height, the wind starts to blow and the parachutist moves horizontally at a velocity of 4.5 m/s as he continues to fall. Use a graphical method to add together the horizontal and the vertical velocities to determine the size of the resultant velocity of the parachutist. Label the velocities and state the scale that you use.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a correct rectangle or triangle with at least one velocity labelled. Award 1 mark for the size of the resultant velocity between 7.8 m/s and 8.0 m/s inclusive. Award 1 mark for stating the scale used.

Variant_1.question_text: State what, for a thermocouple thermometer, is meant by a linear output.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the output, voltage, or e.m.f. is directly proportional to the temperature difference.

Variant_1.question_text: The junction inside the furnace is at temperature T. Fig. 2.2 shows the reading V of the voltmeter at two values of T that are within the operating temperature range of the furnace. When the furnace is operating at a high temperature, the furnace door is opened and the reading of the voltmeter falls to 6.80 mV. Calculate the temperature of the junction inside the furnace.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the calculation method: (800 - 750) / (7.70 - 6.20) or equivalent to find the rate, then use it to calculate from 6.80 mV. Award 1 mark for the temperature as 770 °C.

Variant_1.question_text: State why a liquid-in-glass thermometer is not used to measure the temperature inside the furnace.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following: the glass melts, the liquid boils, or there is no remote reading such as the head being in the furnace.

Variant_1.question_text: The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. The motor lifts bricks of total mass 54 kg through a height of 2.8 m in 3.0 s. Calculate the work done in lifting the bricks.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the calculation method: work done equals mass times gravitational field strength times height, or 54 × 10 × 2.8. Award 1 mark for the work done as 1500 J, 1510 J, or 1512 J.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the useful output power of the motor.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the calculation method: power equals work done divided by time, or the value from (a)(i) divided by 3.0 s. Award 1 mark for the power as 500 W, 503 W, or 504 W.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest two reasons why the electrical power supplied to the motor is greater than its useful output power.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for any two of the following: the motor also lifts the board or rope; there is heat in the motor, wires, or cable; or there is friction with something named, such as the axle, spindle, or air.

Variant_1.question_text: Fig. 3.2 shows a battery and an electric motor. The battery is used to power the motor. Complete Fig. 3.2 to show the circuit diagram of a circuit that is used to determine the power supplied to the electric motor.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for showing the power supply, motor, and ammeter in series. Award 1 mark for showing a voltmeter to measure the voltage across the motor.

Variant_1.question_text: State how the power supplied is calculated.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the power supplied is calculated as current reading times voltage reading, or as VI.

Variant_1.question_text: A petrol tanker has mass 2800 kg when empty and holds 30 m³ of petrol when full. The density of petrol is 740 kg/m³. Calculate the total mass of the tanker when full of petrol.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the calculation method: mass equals density times volume, or 740 × 30. Award 1 mark for the total mass as 25 000 kg or 2.5 × 10⁴ kg, allowing 24 800 kg from 22 000 kg.

Variant_1.question_text: The full tanker is travelling along a road when the driver observes an obstruction and applies the brakes. A resultant force of 30 000 N acts on the tanker. Calculate the deceleration of the tanker.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the calculation method: deceleration equals force divided by mass, or 30 000 divided by the mass from (a). Award 1 mark for the deceleration as 1.2 m/s².

Variant_1.question_text: A water wave of frequency 3.6 Hz is generated in a ripple tank. Fig. 5.1 is a scale diagram that shows the wavefronts travelling from deep water to shallow water in the ripple tank. A length of 1.0 cm on the diagram represents 8.0 cm in the ripple tank. Using measurements from Fig. 5.1, determine the wavelength of the wave in the deep water.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a clear attempt at measuring more than one wavelength, such as 6.85 cm or 5 on the diagram, leading to 1.30–1.45 cm. Award 1 mark for the wavelength as 10.7 cm to 11.3 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the speed of the wave in the deep water.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the calculation method: speed equals frequency times wavelength, or 3.6 Hz times the wavelength from (a)(i). Award 1 mark for the speed as 40 cm/s or 39.6 cm/s.

Variant_1.question_text: As the wave passes into the shallow water, state what happens to the frequency.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the frequency stays the same.

Variant_1.question_text: As the wave passes into the shallow water, state what happens to the speed.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the speed decreases.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe an experiment to verify the principle of moments. You may include a diagram in your answer.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Mentioning appropriate apparatus, for example, a ruler, weights, and a fulcrum. Award 1 mark for: Describing the action, for example, balancing weights on each side. Award 1 mark for: Stating one of the following: the force or mass times distance, or calculating the moment. Award 1 mark for: Indicating to vary or repeat the experiment.

Variant_2.question_text: Fig. 1.1 shows a spanner tightening a nut. A force of 8.0 N is applied to the spanner at a perpendicular distance of 0.15 m from the centre of the nut. Calculate the moment of the force acting on the nut.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award the first mark for: Using the formula for moment as force times distance, for example, 8.0 × 0.15. Award the second mark for: The correct answer of 1.2 N m (and specify that J is not accepted).

Variant_2.question_text: The gravitational field strength is 10 N / kg. Calculate the mass of the 45 N load.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation of mass as 4.5 kg.

Variant_2.question_text: Use the grid in Fig. 2.1 to plot the extension-load graph for this wire for a load between 0 and 45 N.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Axes labelled with the correct quantity and unit. Award 1 mark for: Using a linear scale. Award 1 mark for: Drawing a straight line from the origin (0,0) to the correct point.

Variant_2.question_text: Use the graph plotted in Fig. 2.1 to determine the load needed to produce an extension of 1.3 mm.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Determining the load from the candidate's plotted line on the graph.

Variant_2.question_text: The gravitational field strength is 10 N / kg. Calculate the change in his gravitational potential energy as he falls through 20 m.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award the first mark for: Using the formula for potential energy change as mass times gravitational field strength times height, for example, 75 × 10 × 20. Award the second mark for: The correct answer of 1.5 × 10^4 J.

Variant_2.question_text: When he is 20 m below the platform, his kinetic energy is equal to the change in his gravitational potential energy calculated in (a)(i). Calculate his speed at this point.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award the first mark for: Using the formula for kinetic energy as 1/2 mass times velocity squared, for example, 1/2 × 75 × v^2. Award the second mark for: Correctly deriving v^2 = 400. Award the third mark for: The correct answer of 20 m/s.

Variant_2.question_text: State the energy changes that take place as he falls from 20 m to 25 m below the platform. Ignore the effect of air resistance.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Gravitational potential energy at the start. Award 1 mark for: Kinetic energy at the start. Award 1 mark for: Conversion to elastic potential energy or strain energy at the end (not stretch energy, and no intermediate energy).

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the average force exerted on the piston by the gas during compression.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award the first mark for: Using the formula force equals pressure times area, for example, 4.6 × 10^5 × 0.005. Award the second mark for: The correct answer of 2300 N.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the work done on the gas during compression.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award the first mark for: Using the formula work done equals force times distance, for example, 2300 × 0.074. Award the second mark for: The correct answer of 170 J (or 170.2 J).

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the maximum possible temperature rise of the gas.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award the first mark for: Using the formula temperature rise equals heat energy divided by heat capacity, for example, 170 / 0.27. Award the second mark for: The correct answer of 630 °C (or 629.6 °C).

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a reason why the actual temperature rise of the gas is less than the value calculated in (b)(i).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Thermal energy or heat lost to the cylinder, environment, or atmosphere, or work done against friction.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why no thermal energy (heat) is transferred from the surface of the Sun to the Earth by either conduction or convection.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Space is a vacuum or empty. Award 1 mark for: These methods require matter, a medium, or molecules, and do not occur in a vacuum.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain how painting the houses white helps to maintain a comfortable temperature both during the day and during the night.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 3 marks for any three of the following: During the day: White is a poor absorber or good reflector of radiation, so less heat is absorbed or less heating of the house occurs. During the night: White is a poor emitter or radiator, so less heat is emitted or heat loss from the house is reduced. Anywhere: This applies to infrared radiation or radiant heat.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure 80 cm3 of water from the supply, using the measuring cylinder. This water has a mass mw = 80 g. Pour the water into the cup. Measure the temperature θ1 of the water. θ1 =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording a sensible temperature for θ1, measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, and including the unit (degrees Celsius).

Variant_1.question_text: Take a spoonful of ice, pour off excess water and dry the ice with a paper towel. Place the ice into the cup. Stir the mixture and note the temperature when all the ice has melted. Continue adding spoonfuls of dried ice and stirring until the temperature of the water after the ice has melted is below 15 °C. Record the final temperature θ2 of the water. θ2 =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording a sensible temperature for θ2 that is less than 15 °C, measured to the nearest degree Celsius or better, and including the unit (degrees Celsius). Note that only one penalty for missing or incorrect units applies across the question.

Variant_1.question_text: Carefully pour the water from the cup into the empty measuring cylinder and determine the final volume of water. Final volume of water =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly determining the final volume of water, where the volume of water from melted ice is calculated as final volume minus initial volume (80 cm3), and ensuring that the mass is numerically equal to the volume, with units of volume and mass appropriately used.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the volume of water produced from the melted ice. Volume of water from melted ice =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is part of the marking for (b)(i). Award marks as specified in (b)(i) for the calculation, ensuring the volume is correctly derived as final volume minus initial volume.

Variant_1.question_text: A volume of 1.0 cm3 of water has a mass of 1.0 g. Calculate the mass mi of ice that was added to the water. mi =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the mass mi of ice, which is numerically equal to the volume of water from melted ice, ensuring units are included as per the instructions.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the thermal energy (heat) Q1 lost by the water, initially at room temperature, using the relationship Q1 = mw cw (θ1 – θ2) where cw = 4.2 J / (g °C) and mw = 80 g. Q1 =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating Q1 using the formula Q1 = mw × cw × (θ1 – θ2), where mw = 80 g and cw = 4.2 J/(g °C), resulting in a value approximately 5000 J.

Variant_1.question_text: Using your value from (b)(iii), calculate the thermal energy Q2 gained by the water formed from the melted ice, using the relationship Q2 = mi cw θ2. Q2 =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating Q2 using the formula Q2 = mi × cw × θ2, where mi is from (b)(iii) and cw = 4.2 J/(g °C), resulting in a value approximately 1000 J.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the specific latent heat of fusion L of ice using the relationship L = (Q1 – Q2) / mi. L =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating L using the formula L = (Q1 – Q2) / mi, with the unit (J/g), resulting in a value approximately 250 J/g.

Variant_1.question_text: Connect the crocodile clips between two points that are 1.00 m apart on wire A. Measure the current I in the circuit and the potential difference (p.d.) V across the 1.00 m length of wire A. Immediately disconnect the crocodile clips from the wire. I =  V =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring the current I in the range 0.08 A to 0.20 A, to a precision of 0.01 A or better, with the unit (amperes). Award another 1 mark for measuring the potential difference V in the range 0.40 V to 0.90 V, to a precision of 0.01 V or better, with the unit (volts).

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the resistance RA of wire A, using the relationship RA = V / I. RA =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating RA using the values of V and I from (a), expressed to 2 significant figures, and including the unit (ohms).

Variant_1.question_text: Connect the crocodile clips between two points that are 1.00 m apart on wire B. Determine the resistance RB of wire B, using the method described in (a) and (b). RB =
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring a current less than that in (a), a potential difference greater than that in (a), and correctly calculating RB using the method in (a) and (b), expressed to 2 significant figures, with the unit (ohms).

Variant_1.question_text: The ratio cross-sectional area of wire A / cross-sectional area of wire B is given on the card supplied by the Supervisor. Theory states that cross-sectional area of wire A / cross-sectional area of wire B = RB / RA. Suggest whether your results support this theory.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating the resistance ratio RB / RA and making a sensible comment, such as stating that it is approximately equal to the given ratio, indicating support for the theory.

Variant_1.question_text: Set up the apparatus as shown in Fig. 3.1. The illuminated object should be at the 0.0 cm end of the rule and the screen should be at the 100.0 cm end. Adjust the position of the lens to produce an image on the screen that is smaller than the object. Describe the technique that you use to obtain an image that is in sharp focus.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing a technique to obtain a sharp image, such as approaching the sharp focus from both directions, ensuring the center of the object and the center of the lens are co-linear and parallel.

Variant_1.question_text: The distance between the object and the lens is u and the distance between the lens and the screen is v.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that u + v = 100 ± 1 cm and u > v, with at least one distance measured to the nearest millimeter or better, including the unit (centimeters), and ensuring u is in the range 78.0 cm to 85.0 cm and v is in the range 15.0 cm to 22.0 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: For the next part, adjust to produce an image larger than the object. (Note: The question text is incomplete in the provided material, so based on the mark scheme, this refers to measuring u and v for v > u.)
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that u + v = 100 ± 1 cm and v > u, with at least one distance measured to the nearest millimeter or better, including the unit (centimeters), and ensuring u is in the range 15.0 cm to 22.0 cm and v is in the range 78.0 cm to 85.0 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Set up the apparatus as shown in Fig. 1.1.

Lower the boss holding the rod so that it is as low as possible on the stand. Use some of the Blu-Tack to prevent the end of the ramp sliding across the bench. Place the wooden block at the top of the ramp with face A downwards. Raise the boss to slowly raise one end of the ramp. As you slowly raise the ramp, keep giving the wooden block small pushes until it moves down the ramp at approximately constant speed. When this happens tighten the boss to the stand so that the ramp is fixed in position. This is shown in Fig. 1.2.

The distances x and y are the horizontal and vertical distances between the ends of the ramp, as shown in Fig. 1.2. Measure x and y. x =  ...................................................... y =  ......................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: One of x or y measured to the nearest millimetre or better with the unit, and neither greater than 30.0 cm.
Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation of y / x with no unit.

Award 1 mark for: If x is greater than the previous x in (b), then y must be less than the previous y in (b), or vice versa, and one of the values measured to the nearest millimetre or better with the unit, with the correct y / x with no unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Using your two values for y / x, calculate the average value for y / x. average value for y / x =  ..................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The correct average value of y / x with no unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Use a piece of Blu-Tack to secure the 200 g mass to the wooden block. Repeat (b) and (c) to obtain a second average value for y / x. average value for y / x =  ..................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Two or more values for x and y, and the average y / x within 0.10 of the previous value in (c)(ii), with if the second x is greater than the first x, then the second y must be less than the first y, or vice versa.
Award 1 mark for: A simple comparison of the ratio values in (c)(ii) and (d), for example, one value is greater than the other, with a consistent conclusion, for example, the theory is not supported.

Variant_2.question_text: Connect the voltmeter in parallel with the LED. Close the switch and measure the potential difference (p.d.) Vd across the LED. Vd =  ..................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Vd in the range 1.4 to 2.0 V, measured to 0.01 V or better with the unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Open the switch and disconnect the voltmeter. Connect the voltmeter in parallel with the resistor. Close the switch and measure the p.d. Vr across the resistor. Vr =  ..................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Vr in the range 0.5 to 1.5 V, measured to 0.01 V or better with the unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Open the switch and disconnect the voltmeter. Calculate the current I in the circuit using the relationship I = Vr / R, where R is the resistance of the resistor that is in the circuit, in this case 100 Ω. I =  ..................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation of I to at least two significant figures with the unit.
Award 1 mark for: A decrease in Vd not greater than 0.20 V and an increase in Vr not greater than 0.3 V, and the correct calculation of I to at least two significant figures with the unit.

Award 1 mark for: A comment on both voltages and current, for example, small change in the p.d. across each component even though there is a large drop in the current, or Vd decreases, Vr increases and I decreases, or Vd + Vr is constant or increases slightly, or the resistance of the LED increases so the LED is non-ohmic.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain how you ensured that the metre rule was vertical. You may add to Fig. 3.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Checked with set-square against bench and metre rule, or aligned with a vertical in the room such as the edge of a window frame.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: e in the range 5.0 cm to 9.0 cm, measured to the nearest millimetre or better with the unit.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The correct calculation of C to at least two significant figures.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: New e less than the extension in (b) and in the range 2.0 cm to 5.0 cm, measured to the nearest millimetre or better with the unit.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Total mass of modelling clay and hook in the range 100 g to 130 g with the unit, based on the correct new e and C.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1c, draw the mass at its highest position after release.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: drawing the mass at a horizontal level or at a point marked level with the top of the hook.

Variant_1.question_text: Mark the most suitable position for the student’s eye when the student times the vibrations.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: marking the eye or E labelled level with a dotted line.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why the mass is pulled down only a small distance before being released.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: the extension is within the elastic limit of the spring, or the spring is not permanently stretched, or it gives smooth oscillations, or the load does not jump off the spring, or the spring does not become slack.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest a reason why the student times twenty vibrations rather than one.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: it reduces human reaction error in measuring T, or T is too small to measure accurately, or it gives an average value of T, or it provides a more accurate measurement of T.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the average value of T, the time for one complete vibration.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: correctly calculating the sum of the times and dividing by 10, such as 8.024 or 8.02 or 8.0 seen, or Σt ÷ 10. Award 1 mark for: the final answer of 0.4012 or 0.401 or 0.40 seconds.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.2, write your value of T for W = 1.0 N. Use a suitable number of significant figures.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: writing 0.401 in the table, based on the calculated value from part (d), using three significant figures.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: axes correctly labelled with quantity and unit, and the correct way round. Award 1 mark for: scales that are linear, not awkward, and use more than half the grid, for example, x-axis: 2 cm ≡ 1 N, y-axis: 2 cm ≡ 0.1 s. Award 1 mark for: points plotted accurately within half a small square, using neat crosses or small points in a circle. Award 1 mark for: a smooth curve of best fit neatly drawn.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: the lamp lights up at normal brightness.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of: broken wire, connections not good, lamp blown or faulty, or cell(s) run down.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe what the student observes as the crocodile clips are slowly moved further apart on the wire.

Variant_1.question_text: Draw its circuit symbol.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain how the component is made small.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why the bell-jar must have thick walls.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why sound can be heard when the bell-jar is touched.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why a rubber-soled shoe gives a larger angle than a smooth-soled shoe.

Part C: Award 1 mark for points plotted accurately within half a small square, using neat crosses or small points in a circle. Part D: Award 1 mark for drawing a smooth curve of best fit.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a possible reason why the student cannot take readings for d greater than 3.0 cm.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: The tray is not deep enough; or water spills out when the wave is made; or the time is too small; or the wave travels too fast.

Variant_2.question_text: Using your graph, suggest how the speed varies with depth for very large values of d.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: The speed becomes constant and does not vary with depth; or the increase in speed becomes smaller with increasing depth; or the gradient decreases with increasing depth.

Variant_2.question_text: The relationship power × time = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change is used to find the specific heat capacity of water. Use the relationship and the given data to find the smallest possible value for the specific heat capacity c of water. Give your answer to a suitable number of significant figures.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Part A: Award 1 mark for correctly using 2755 in the calculation or obtaining 34. Part B: Award 1 mark for obtaining 4861.8 or any number of significant figures. Part C: Award 1 mark for the final answer of 4860 or 4900, with no unit required.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the water should be stirred before reading the thermometer.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: To ensure uniform temperature or heat distribution throughout the water.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the thermometer should not touch the metal plate when measuring the temperature of the water.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The base of the kettle is hotter than the water, so the thermometer would measure a higher temperature than that of the water.

Variant_2.question_text: The student’s answer for c is larger than the accepted value. Suggest a reason why this answer is too large.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: There are heat losses to the kettle, surroundings, or to evaporate water; or the power used is too large; or the time is too large; or the mass is too small; or the temperature difference is too small.

Variant_1.question_text: Forces of 3 N and 4 N act as shown in the diagram. Which diagram shows the resultant R of these two forces? A R 3 N 4 N B R 3 N 4 N C R 3 N 4 N D R 3 N 4 N
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which correctly shows the resultant force vector.

Variant_1.question_text: A micrometer is used to measure the diameter of a uniform wire. What is done to obtain an accurate answer? A Find the reading and add or subtract the zero error. B Make the micrometer horizontal. C Subtract the fixed scale reading from the rotating scale reading. D Subtract the rotating scale reading from the fixed scale reading.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which involves correcting the measurement by accounting for the zero error.

Variant_1.question_text: The speed-time graph for a falling skydiver is shown below. As he falls, the skydiver spreads out his arms and legs and then opens his parachute. Which part of the graph shows the skydiver falling with terminal velocity? speed time A B C D
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which indicates the portion of the graph where speed is constant, representing terminal velocity.

Variant_1.question_text: A particle P is moving in a horizontal circle about O. P moves at constant speed. Which statement is true? A A force of constant size acts on P in the direction of motion. B A force of constant size acts on P towards O. C The force on P varies in size as it moves around the circle. D There is no resultant force acting on P.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B, which states that a centripetal force of constant size acts towards the center O.

Variant_1.question_text: A body slides down a frictionless slope as shown. As the body presses on the surface, the surface pushes back on the body. The force of the surface on the body is sometimes called the reaction force. In which direction does the reaction force act? A direction of motion B C D
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which shows the reaction force perpendicular to the surface of the slope.

Variant_1.question_text: Paper is sold in packets labelled 80 g / m2. This means that a sheet of paper of area 10 000 cm2 has a mass of 80 g. The thickness of each sheet is 0.11 mm. What is the density of the paper? A 0.073 g / cm3 B 0.088 g / cm3 C 0.73 g / cm3 D 0.88 g / cm3
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which gives the correct density calculated from the mass, area, and thickness.

Variant_1.question_text: Which chair is the least stable if the child moves? A B C D
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which represents the chair with the highest center of mass or least base stability.

Variant_1.question_text: A brick of weight 80 N stands upright on the ground as shown. What is the pressure it exerts on the ground? A 0.080 N / cm2 B 0.40 N / cm2 C 0.80 N / cm2 D 1.6 N / cm2
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which correctly calculates pressure as force divided by the contact area.

Variant_1.question_text: An airtight container holds a fixed mass of gas. Its pressure and volume are measured on four occasions when the temperature is 20 °C. The results are shown in the table. Three sets of readings are correct. Which set of readings is not correct? pressure / kPa volume / cm3 A 120 36 B 100 48 C 80 60 D 60 80
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which does not follow Boyle's law for the given conditions.

Variant_1.question_text: Energy is released in some power stations and in the Sun by either nuclear fission or nuclear fusion. Which type of nuclear reaction applies in each case? power station Sun A fission fission B fission fusion C fusion fission D fusion fusion
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B, which correctly identifies fission for power stations and fusion for the Sun.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a girl lifting a box of weight 50 N from a low shelf to a high shelf. How much work is done on the box? A 30 J B 60 J C 90 J D 120 J
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B, which calculates work as force times vertical distance moved.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows the energy transfer through a machine. The machine is 50% efficient. Which equation is correct? A input energy = useful output energy B useful output energy = input energy + wasted energy C wasted energy = input energy + useful output energy D wasted energy = useful output energy
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which states that wasted energy equals useful output energy for 50% efficiency.

Variant_1.question_text: A glass beaker contains water. When the centre of the base of the beaker is heated, a convection current is set up. Which statement explains this? A The evaporation of water causes water molecules to rise to the surface. B The expansion of water molecules causes them to rise to the surface. C The water above the heat source rises because it becomes less dense. D The water at the sides sinks because it becomes less dense.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which explains that heated water rises due to decreased density.

Variant_1.question_text: Two identical metal plates are painted, one matt white and the other matt black. These are placed at equal distances from a radiant heater as shown. The heater is turned on for five minutes. Which metal plate absorbs more energy and which plate emits more energy in this time? absorbs more emits more A black black B black white C white black D white white
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which correctly identifies that the black plate absorbs and emits more energy.

Variant_1.question_text: Using an electric kettle, 200 g of water at 100 °C is converted into steam at 100 °C in 300 seconds. The specific latent heat of steam is 2250 J / g. What is the average electrical power used? A 200 / 300 × 2250 W B 300 / 2250 × 200 W C 200 / 2250 × 300 W D 200 × 300 × 2250 W
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which correctly calculates power as energy transferred divided by time.

Variant_1.question_text: When a gas in a container of fixed volume is heated, the pressure of the gas increases. Which statement explains this? A The molecules expand and get heavier, so they hit each other harder. B The molecules have less room to move, so they collide with each other more frequently. C The molecules hit the walls harder and less frequently. D The molecules move faster, so they collide with the walls more frequently.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which explains that increased molecular speed leads to more frequent collisions with the walls.

Variant_1.question_text: A ripple tank is used to show wave behaviour. The dipper vibrates up and down at a constant frequency. What happens to the frequency and to the speed of the wave as it reaches the glass block? frequency speed A decreases decreases B decreases increases C remains the same decreases D remains the same increases
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which states that frequency remains the same while speed decreases in the glass block.

Variant_1.question_text: A wave has a frequency of 10 kHz. Which pair of values of its speed and wavelength is possible? speed / m s–1 wavelength / m A 100 0.001 B 340 0.034 C 1500 150 D 3000 300
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which provides a possible speed and wavelength pair based on the wave equation.

Variant_2.question_text: Forces of 3 N and 4 N act as shown in the diagram. Which diagram shows the resultant R of these two forces?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which correctly identifies the diagram showing the resultant force of the 3 N and 4 N forces.

Variant_2.question_text: Before marking the finishing line on a running track, a groundsman measures out its 100 m length. Which instrument is the most appropriate for this purpose?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which is measuring tape, the most appropriate instrument for measuring 100 m.

Variant_2.question_text: The speed-time graph for a falling skydiver is shown below. As he falls, the skydiver spreads out his arms and legs and then opens his parachute. Which part of the graph shows the skydiver falling with terminal velocity?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which correctly identifies the part of the graph where the speed is constant, indicating terminal velocity.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows the speed-time graph of the motion of a car for four seconds. What is the distance travelled by the car in the four seconds?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B, which is 25 m, calculated as the area under the speed-time graph.

Variant_2.question_text: A car of mass 1500 kg travels along a horizontal road. It accelerates steadily from 10 m/s to 25 m/s in 5.0 s. What is the force needed to produce this acceleration?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which is 4500 N, calculated using F = m × a, where acceleration a = (25 - 10) / 5.0 = 3 m/s².

Variant_2.question_text: Balanced forces are acting on a moving body. What happens to the direction of movement and to the speed of the body?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which states that the direction of movement does not change and the speed does not change, as balanced forces mean no net force acts on the body.

Variant_2.question_text: A particle P is moving in a horizontal circle about O. P moves at constant speed. Which statement is true?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B, which states that a force of constant size acts on P towards O, representing the centripetal force required for circular motion.

Variant_2.question_text: A measuring cylinder contains 20 cm³ of water. A stone is placed in the water and the water level rises to 38 cm³. What is the volume of the stone?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which is 18 cm³, calculated as the difference in water levels (38 cm³ - 20 cm³).

Variant_2.question_text: Which chair is the least stable if the child moves?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which identifies the chair with the highest center of mass or narrowest base, making it the least stable.

Variant_2.question_text: The graph shows extension-load curves for four fibres. Which fibre is the hardest to stretch over the range of loads shown?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which corresponds to the fibre with the steepest slope on the extension-load graph, indicating it requires the most load to stretch.

Variant_2.question_text: Four beakers contain the same liquid. At which point is the pressure greatest?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which is at the deepest point in the liquid, as pressure increases with depth.

Variant_2.question_text: An airtight container holds a fixed mass of gas. Its pressure and volume are measured on four occasions when the temperature is 20 °C. The results are shown in the table. Three sets of readings are correct. Which set of readings is not correct?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which does not follow Boyle's law (P × V = constant at constant temperature), as the product P × V is not consistent with the other options.

Variant_2.question_text: A swimmer dives into a very deep pool at high speed. He slows down as he moves towards the bottom of the pool. What is the overall energy transformation as the diver moves downwards through the water?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which describes the transformation from kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy to thermal energy due to friction and slowing down.

Variant_2.question_text: A lorry of mass 10 000 kg takes 5000 kg of sand to the top of a hill 50 m high, unloads the sand and then returns to the bottom of the hill. The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. What is the overall gain in potential energy?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option C, which is 2 500 000 J, calculated as the gain in potential energy for the lorry only (mgh = 10 000 kg × 10 N/kg × 50 m), since the sand is unloaded.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows the energy transfer through a machine. The machine is 50% efficient. Which equation is correct?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which states that wasted energy equals useful output energy, as efficiency is 50%, meaning wasted energy = useful output energy.

Variant_2.question_text: Two identical metal plates are painted, one matt white and the other matt black. These are placed at equal distances from a radiant heater as shown. The heater is turned on for five minutes. Which metal plate absorbs more energy and which plate emits more energy in this time?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which states that the black plate absorbs more energy and emits more energy, due to its properties as a better absorber and emitter of radiation.

Variant_2.question_text: A liquid-in-glass thermometer contains mercury. Which physical property of the mercury varies with temperature, enabling the thermometer to operate?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option D, which is volume, as the mercury expands with temperature, allowing the thermometer to measure it.

Variant_2.question_text: Thermal energy of 12 000 J is supplied to a 2.0 kg mass of copper. The specific heat capacity of copper is 400 J/(kg °C). What is the rise in temperature?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which is 15 °C, calculated using Q = m c ΔT, so ΔT = Q / (m c) = 12 000 / (2.0 × 400).

Variant_2.question_text: Using an electric kettle, 200 g of water at 100 °C is converted into steam at 100 °C in 300 seconds. The specific latent heat of steam is 2250 J/g. What is the average electrical power used?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option B, which is (200 × 2250) / 300 W, calculated as energy supplied divided by time.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a fixed mass of gas in a cylinder fitted with a piston that can move easily. What is the change, if any, in the pressure and volume of the gas after it is heated?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting option A, which states that pressure does not change and volume increases, as heating causes expansion at constant pressure if the piston moves freely.

Variant_1.question_text: Determine the length of the unstretched spring.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the length is 11 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain how the graph shows that the limit of proportionality is not reached.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the graph is a straight line, linear, or has a constant gradient, or is not curved.

Variant_1.question_text: State what is meant by a uniform acceleration.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that uniform acceleration means a change in speed or velocity. Award 1 mark for stating that the change is the same in the same time or in 1 second.

Variant_1.question_text: The extension of the spring is 9.0 cm. Using Fig. 1.1, determine the horizontal force on M.
