Variant_1.question_text: The extension of the spring is 9.0 cm. Using Fig. 1.1, determine the acceleration of M.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula acceleration equals force divided by mass, or a numerical calculation such as 0.8 divided by 0.2. Award 1 mark for the correct acceleration of 4.0 m/s².

Variant_1.question_text: State the principle of moments for a body in equilibrium.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that for a body in equilibrium, the total or resultant moment is zero, or the sum of clockwise moments equals the sum of anticlockwise moments.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the reading on the newton meter.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the equation force times distance, such as F1 times d1 or F2 times d2. Award 1 mark for the correct reading of 8.0 N.

Variant_1.question_text: The weight of the metre rule is 1.2 N. Calculate the size of the force exerted on the metre rule by the pivot.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for adding 4 N and 1.2 N to get 5.2 N. Award 1 mark for the correct force of 2.8 N, or an error-carried-forward from part (b) such as 5.2 N minus the value from (b).

Variant_1.question_text: Three points on Fig. 3.1 are labelled P, Q and R. State which two of these points are at atmospheric pressure.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that points Q and R are at atmospheric pressure.

Variant_1.question_text: The density of mercury is 13600 kg/m³ and the gravitational field strength g is 10 N/kg. Calculate the pressure due to the column of mercury of length 0.76 m.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula pressure equals density times gravitational field strength times height, in algebraic or numerical form. Award 1 mark for the correct pressure of 1.0336 × 10⁵ N/m².

Variant_1.question_text: State and explain what happens if the mercury in the apparatus shown in Fig. 3.1 is replaced with water.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that water is less dense or has a density of 1000 kg/m³. Award 1 mark for stating that water will rise further up the tube, fill the tube, have a greater height, or enter the pump.

Variant_1.question_text: State the range of the thermometer shown in Fig. 4.1.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the range is 120 °C or from –10 °C to 110 °C.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how Fig. 4.1 shows that the thermometer has a linear scale.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the thermometer has the same distance or length on the scale for a temperature rise, or has regular intervals or equal divisions along the scale.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 4.3, draw a more sensitive scale.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a scale on Fig. 4.3 with any two markings further apart and none less apart than on the original scale.

Variant_1.question_text: State one other property, apart from volume or expansion, which can be used to measure temperature.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating a property such as resistance of a metal, electromotive force/voltage/current/potential difference of a thermocouple, pressure of a gas, colour, or quantity of radiation.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain how Fig. 1.1 shows that the ball is accelerating.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The ball travels further in each second or in the same time interval between the images.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the average speed of the ball between t = 1.0 s and 3.0 s.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula for speed as distance divided by time. Award 1 mark for the correct answer: 40 cm/s or 0.40 m/s.
Award 1 mark for: Air resistance increases. Award 1 mark for: Weight remains constant.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why, if the slope is long enough, the ball eventually reaches a constant speed.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The forces balance or cancel out, resulting in no net force or resultant force.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain what is meant by the moment of a force.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The moment of a force is the force multiplied by the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of action of the force. Award 1 mark for a clear explanation including the concept of force times perpendicular distance.

Variant_2.question_text: By taking moments about A, calculate T.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the principle of moments, such as T multiplied by 8.0 m equals 2000 N multiplied by 2.0 m. Award 1 mark for the correct answer: 500 N.

Variant_2.question_text: The force T and the force that the rope exerts on the man are related by Newton’s third law. State the relationship between these forces.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The two forces are equal in magnitude. Award 1 mark for: The two forces are opposite in direction.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the work done on the spring.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula for work done as force multiplied by distance, such as 90 N multiplied by 0.3 m. Award 1 mark for the correct answer: 27 J.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the power used to extend the spring.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula for power as work done divided by time, such as 27 J divided by 3 seconds or equivalent calculation. Award 1 mark for the correct answer: 9.0 W.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the extension of the spring when the force is 800 N.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the proportionality from the graph or Hooke’s law, such as 800 N multiplied by 30 cm divided by 180 N. Award 1 mark for the correct answer: 133 cm or 130 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest what happens to the extension.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The extension is more than 143 cm or there is some permanent extension.

Variant_2.question_text: Arrange the components in order of increasing wavelength.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the correct order: gamma rays, visible light, infra-red.

Variant_2.question_text: State which component of the electromagnetic spectrum is used to transmit the television signal to the satellite.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Microwaves.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain how the satellite is used.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The satellite receives and sends, transmits, emits, boosts, or amplifies the signal. Additionally, award 1 mark for: It covers a large area over the horizon, is unaffected by tall buildings or hills, or requires only one transmitter.

Variant_1.question_text: Determine accurate values for the length l, the width w and the height h of the stack of slides.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for providing sensible values for l, w, and h within the ranges 73 mm to 79 mm for l, 22 mm to 28 mm for w, and 9 mm to 16 mm for h, measured to the nearest mm, with units. Award 1 mark for repeating the measurements of l, w, and h.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure the mass M of the stack of slides.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring the mass M of the stack of slides with a unit and using it correctly in the density calculation, where M must be in one of the following ranges depending on the height h of the stack: for h between 9 mm and 10 mm, M between 43 g and 51 g; for h between 10 mm and 11 mm, M between 49 g and 55 g; for h between 11 mm and 12 mm, M between 52 g and 60 g; for h between 12 mm and 13 mm, M between 57 g and 65 g; for h between 13 mm and 14 mm, M between 60 g and 69 g; for h between 14 mm and 15 mm, M between 64 g and 76 g.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the density ρ of the stack of slides using the formula ρ = M / (l × w × h).
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating the density ρ using the formula ρ = M / (l × w × h) and including the unit, where M is as specified in part (b)(i).

Variant_1.question_text: Write down the values of t and m that are on the card.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks as part of the overall scheme; this sub-part is not separately marked in the scheme provided.

Variant_1.question_text: Estimate the number of slides in the stack by two methods.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the method: correctly calculating the number of slides from M divided by m and from h divided by t, with clear working allowing non-integer values.

Variant_1.question_text: Comment on the values obtained in (ii). Explain your answer.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for comments such as: the values should be integers so if the ratios are not integers, all slides are not identical; or the ratios should give the same answer if slides are not identical because mass is proportional to thickness; or the values are not integers due to measurement errors; or if integers, the slides in the stack are the same as the single slide; or the tape does not affect mass and thickness equally.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 2.2, draw a normal to the line XY at the point M and below the line XY.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a normal to the line XY at point M below the line XY that is correct by eye.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 2.2, draw a line from M at an angle of 40° to the normal, towards the bottom left-hand side of the page. Label this line L.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark as part of the overall for (a); included in the mark for drawing the angle of incidence of 40° correctly by eye.

Variant_1.question_text: Place the front surface of the mirror along the line XY with the reflective surface facing the bottom of the page. Position the light source and slit so that a ray of light passes along the line L towards M.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark as part of (b)(ii); not separately marked.

Variant_1.question_text: Mark two points on the reflected ray. Choose the points so that the reflected ray can be drawn as accurately as possible. Label the points P1 and P2.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for marking two points on the reflected ray such that one is within 5.0 cm of the line indicating the column 'For examiners use only' and the other is within 2.0 cm of the front of the mirror, both measured along the ray, and the position is approximately correct.

Variant_1.question_text: Remove the mirror. Draw a line through P1 and P2 that extends above XY. Label this line R1.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark as part of the overall for (c); not separately marked in the scheme.

Place the front of the mirror along this new line with the reflective surface facing the bottom of the page. Again position the light source and slit so that a ray of light passes along line L towards M.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for marking the new position of the front of the mirror on the diagram.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 2.2, mark two points on the ray reflected from the mirror in its new position. Label these points P3 and P4.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for marking two points on the new reflected ray that is approximately correct, about 10° below the horizontal (allowing up to about 20° below by eye).

Variant_1.question_text: Draw a line through P3 and P4 that meets the line R1. Label this line R2.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark as part of the overall for (c); not separately marked.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure the angle θ between the lines R1 and R2.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring θ between 36° and 44° from the correct diagram.

Variant_1.question_text: In the space below, draw a circuit diagram of the circuit.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a circuit diagram showing the power supply, resistor, and capacitor in series, with the switch in parallel with the capacitor and the voltmeter in parallel with the resistor.

Variant_1.question_text: Close the switch to discharge the capacitor. Record the reading V0 on the voltmeter.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording V0 in the range 2.2 V to 3.5 V with a unit.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate V0 / 2.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating V0 / 2 using the recorded V0.

Variant_1.question_text: Open the switch and start the stopwatch. Stop the stopwatch when the reading on the voltmeter is V0 / 2. Close the switch. Determine the average time t taken for the reading on the voltmeter to fall to V0 / 2.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for t in the range 20 s to 45 s from repeat measurements, with the correct average and unit. Award another mark for the method of determining the average time.

Variant_1.question_text: In this experiment, you will investigate the equilibrium of a mass suspended by two springs. You have been provided with a rod to which two springs are attached, a set of slotted masses, a metre rule, and other apparatus as listed.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: For preliminary results: Award 1 mark for l in the range 1.5 cm to 3.0 cm, measured to the nearest mm with unit. Award 1 mark for p greater than y with units. Award 1 mark for L less than p with at least two measurements to the nearest mm. Award 1 mark for correct calculation of x. Award 1 mark for correct calculation of F in the range 0.39 N to 0.59 N with unit. Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the mass M of the half-metre rule using M = (2 m h) / x where the mass m of P is 50 g.

For this sub-part specifically: Acceptable answers include correct substitution into M = (2 × 50 g × h) / x, resulting in M between 30 g and 70 g with unit.

Variant_1.question_text: Which device can be used to measure the thickness of a single sheet of paper? A a metre rule B a micrometer C a plastic ruler D a measuring tape
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. This option is a micrometer, a device precise enough for measuring small thicknesses like that of a single sheet of paper.

Variant_1.question_text: In a test, four students linked the quantities on the left with their units on the right. Which student matched them all correctly? frequency m / s2 J Hz W acceleration latent heat power A frequency m / s2 J Hz W B frequency m / s2 J Hz W C frequency m / s2 J Hz W D
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is A. This option correctly matches: frequency with Hz, acceleration with m / s2, latent heat with J, and power with W.

Variant_1.question_text: The speed-time graph represents a short journey. Which distance-time graph represents the same journey? A 0 0 distance / m time / s B 0 0 distance / m time / s C 0 0 distance / m time / s D 0 0 distance / m time / s
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is A. This option shows the distance-time graph that corresponds to the given speed-time graph, reflecting the same changes in motion over time.

Variant_1.question_text: A boy sits on a playground roundabout. The roundabout carries the boy in a horizontal, anticlockwise circle at a constant speed. D The resultant force is in direction Y.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is D. This option indicates that the resultant force is towards the center of the circle, in direction Y, as required for circular motion.

Variant_1.question_text: A scientist places a rock on a spring balance. She then places the same rock on a beam balance. At the North Pole, the gravitational field strength is greater than at the Equator. She performs the experiment at the North Pole and at the Equator. How do the readings at the North Pole compare with those at the Equator? scale reading on spring balance masses needed on beam balance A different at North Pole different at North Pole B different at North Pole same at North Pole C same at North Pole different at North Pole D same at North Pole same at North Pole
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. This option correctly states that the scale reading on the spring balance is different at the North Pole due to higher gravitational field strength, while the masses needed on the beam balance remain the same as it compares masses.

Variant_1.question_text: What affects the stability of an object? A only its base area and the location of its centre of mass B only its weight and its base area C only the location of its centre of mass D only its weight
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is A. This option identifies that stability is affected by both the base area and the location of the centre of mass.

Variant_1.question_text: Which object behaves as an elastic solid as it deforms? A a bullet as it hits a solid metal wall B a car damaged in a collision C a piece of metal cut by a saw D a football as it is kicked
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is D. This option describes a football, which deforms and returns to its original shape, behaving as an elastic solid.

Variant_1.question_text: A spring extends by 6.0 cm when a 15 N weight is suspended from its lower end. Three of these springs are used as shown to support a 90 N weight. What is the extension of each spring? A 2.0 cm B 12 cm C 36 cm D 110 cm
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. This option gives 12 cm as the extension, based on the proportional extension for the combined springs supporting the weight.

Variant_1.question_text: Objects of different weights are placed on a rigid, horizontal surface. Which row shows the correct pressure acting on the surface? weight / N area in contact / m2 pressure / Pa A 10 0.1 1 B 20 0.2 0.01 C 30 0.1 300 D 40 0.2 8
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is C. This row correctly calculates pressure as force divided by area, giving 300 Pa for a 30 N weight on a 0.1 m2 area.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a simple manometer that contains a liquid. Side X is connected to a gas supply of pressure R. Side Y is open to the atmosphere at pressure S. Which pressure is the length h used to measure? A R B S C R – S D R + S
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is C. This option indicates that h measures the pressure difference R – S, the gauge pressure in the manometer.

Variant_1.question_text: Air is trapped in a cylinder by a piston. The pressure of the air is p and the length of the air column is 20 cm. The piston is moved outwards until the length of the air column has increased by 40 cm. The temperature of the air remains constant. What is the new air pressure? A 2p B 3p C 2p D 3p
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. According to Boyle's Law, with constant temperature, the new pressure is p/3 when the volume triples.

Variant_1.question_text: A student uses a newton meter to pull an object horizontally along rough ground. The student multiplies the newton meter reading by the distance moved by the object. What does this calculation give? A the efficiency of the process B the kinetic energy of the object C the power generated D the work done on the object
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is D. This calculation gives the work done, as it multiplies force by distance.

Variant_1.question_text: A builder carrying six bricks climbs a ladder. Each brick weighs 25 N and the builder climbs a vertical distance of 4.0 m in 8.0 s. What is the average power used to raise the bricks? A 13 W B 75 W C 750 W D 4800 W
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. The average power is calculated as work done divided by time, giving 75 W for raising the bricks.

Variant_1.question_text: A copper rod is heated at one end. Which statement describes how heat transfer occurs in the copper? A Energetic copper molecules move from the cooler end to the hotter end. B Energetic copper molecules move from the hotter end to the cooler end. C Energetic free electrons move from the cooler end to the hotter end. D Energetic free electrons move from the hotter end to the cooler end.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is D. Heat transfer in copper occurs via conduction, with energetic free electrons moving from the hotter end to the cooler end.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a clinical thermometer. Which factor affects the sensitivity of the thermometer? A the constriction B the diameter of the bore C the length of the glass tube D the thickness of the glass tube
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. The diameter of the bore affects sensitivity, as a narrower bore allows for greater expansion visibility.

Variant_1.question_text: A centimetre scale is fixed next to an unmarked mercury-in-glass thermometer. The ice point and the steam point are marked. What is the temperature shown on the thermometer? A 44 °C B 56 °C C 60 °C D 66 °C
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. Based on the markings, the temperature is 56 °C.

Variant_1.question_text: Water is poured into four dishes. In two of the dishes the water has a small surface area and in the other two it has a large surface area. The water in two of the dishes is cool and the water in the other two is warm. From which dish does the water evaporate the quickest?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B. Water evaporates quickest from the dish with warm water and a large surface area.

Variant_2.question_text: When a heavy coin falls a short distance towards the ground it does not reach terminal velocity. Why is this? A The coin has not hit the ground. Variant_2.marking_scheme: The correct answer is D: The weight of the coin is always more than air resistance.

Variant_2.question_text: In a model of an atom, electrons move in circular orbits around a nucleus. Which statement about the electrons is correct? Variant_1.question_text: For the time 0 to 2.0 s, determine the acceleration of the motorcycle.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating acceleration as the change in speed divided by time, using values from the graph such as (final speed minus initial speed) divided by time, for example, (55 m/s - 40 m/s) divided by 2 s. Award 1 mark for the correct acceleration of 7.5 m/s².

Variant_1.question_text: For the time 0 to 2.0 s, determine the resultant force acting on the motorcycle.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula force equals mass times acceleration, for example, 180 kg times 7.5 m/s². Award 1 mark for the correct force of 1300 N, 1350 N, or 1400 N.

Variant_1.question_text: Using Fig. 1.2, describe how the acceleration of the motorcycle changes during this time.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the acceleration decreases to zero.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain, in terms of the forces acting, why the acceleration changes in this way.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for mentioning air resistance, friction, or drag. Award 1 mark for stating that air resistance, friction, or drag increases with speed, or that the resultant force decreases with speed. Award 1 mark for stating that finally, air resistance equals the driving force, or the resultant force is zero.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the weight of the oil in the cylinder.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the principle of moments, for example, force one times distance one equals force two times distance two, such as (0.39 N times 0.40 m) divided by 0.30 m. Award 1 mark for the correct weight of 0.52 N.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the mass of the oil in the cylinder.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the correct mass of 0.052 kg or 52 g.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the density of the oil.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula density equals mass divided by volume, for example, 52 g divided by 60 cm³ or equivalent. Award 1 mark for the correct density of 870 kg/m³, 867 kg/m³, 866.7 kg/m³, or 0.87 g/cm³.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain, in terms of molecules, how the air inside the cylinder exerts a pressure.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that atoms, molecules, or particles move about or collide. Award 1 mark for stating that atoms, molecules, or particles collide or hit the walls or surface of the cylinder. Award 1 mark for stating that the force on the walls causes pressure.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain, in terms of molecules, why the pressure increases.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that atoms, molecules, or particles are closer, more compact, there are more molecules per unit volume, or less space to move. Award 1 mark for stating that there are more collisions with the wall or surface of the chamber, provided that speed or kinetic energy does not change.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe what is meant by wave motion in a ripple tank.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each of any two of the following: transmission of energy; without net movement of the medium; through vibration of particles.

Variant_1.question_text: State what is meant by frequency.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that frequency is the number of complete waves, cycles, or oscillations per unit time or per second.

Variant_1.question_text: State what is meant by wavelength.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that wavelength is the distance between neighbouring waves, or the distance between neighbouring wavefronts, or points of the same phase, or crest to crest, or trough to trough.

Variant_2.question_text: At point P on Fig. 1.1, the line stops being straight and begins to curve. State the name of point P.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the answer: limit of proportionality.

Variant_2.question_text: Using Fig. 1.1, determine the unstretched length of the spring.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the answer: 8.5 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the extension of the spring for a load of 4.0 N.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the answer: an extension between 7.1 cm and 7.3 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: The student attaches a small block of wood to an identical spring. The extension of the spring is 2.7 cm. The gravitational field strength g is 10 N / kg. Calculate the mass of the block of wood.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for correctly calculating the force or using the appropriate extension, such as 4.0 × (2.7 / 7.2) or 5.0 × (2.7 / 9.0) or reading from the graph to get approximately 1.5 N or 11.2 cm; and 1 mark for the final mass between 0.148 kg and 0.152 kg.

Variant_2.question_text: A nurse applies a force of 2.5 N at a point X on the handle, 0.18 m from the axis of the tap. Calculate the maximum moment about the axis that this force can produce.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for using the formula for moment, such as force multiplied by distance or 2.5 × 0.18; and 1 mark for the answer: 0.45 N m.

Variant_2.question_text: The moment produced by the nurse is less than this maximum value. Suggest one reason why this is so.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the reason: the force is not applied at right angles to the tap.

Variant_2.question_text: State how the force needed to operate the tap is affected by the length of the handle.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the statement: a longer handle requires a smaller force for the same moment, or the force and handle length are inversely related or proportional.

Variant_2.question_text: Initially, all the helium is stored in the cylinder at a pressure of 2.0 × 10^7 Pa. When no more balloons can be filled, all the helium occupies a total volume of 1.8 m^3 and is at a pressure of 1.0 × 10^5 Pa. The temperature of the helium has not changed. Calculate, stating the formula used, the initial volume of the helium in the cylinder.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for stating the formula V1 = (p2 × V2) / p1 or pressure is inversely proportional to volume; 1 mark for the calculation: 1.0 × 10^5 × (1.8 / 2.0 × 10^7); and 1 mark for the answer: 9.0 × 10^{-3} m^3 or 9000 cm^3.

Variant_2.question_text: The balloon seller takes the cylinder back to the helium supplier and it is refilled. The mass of helium in the cylinder is 0.30 kg. Calculate the density of helium in the cylinder.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for using the formula density = mass / volume or 0.30 / 9.0 × 10^{-3}; and 1 mark for the answer: 33.333 kg/m^3 or 0.033333 g/cm^3.

Variant_2.question_text: The mass of the empty cylinder is 16 kg. Suggest why weighing the cylinder is not an accurate method of determining the amount of helium supplied.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the suggestion: the mass or weight of the helium is a small fraction of the total mass, or the weighing includes the weight of the cylinder or the mass of air.

Variant_2.question_text: State one way in which the car is gaining thermal energy.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for one of the following: heat gained from burning fuel or combustion; friction between moving parts, with air, or with the road; or from radiation of the Sun.

Variant_2.question_text: State one way in which the car is losing thermal energy.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for one of the following: heat lost to air or surroundings; by convection currents; through exhaust or hot gases or fumes; or by radiation from the hot car.

Variant_2.question_text: The car accelerates up the hill. In addition to the changes in the thermal energy of the car, there are other energy changes taking place. Describe the other energy changes occurring as the car moves.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for chemical energy decreasing at the start; 1 mark for gravitational or potential energy of the car increasing; and 1 mark for kinetic energy of the car or air increasing.

Variant_2.question_text: The temperature of the wax changes as it cools to room temperature of 23 °C. On the axes below, sketch the graph of temperature against time for the wax as it cools.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for a downward curve of correct curvature from 90 °C; 1 mark for a horizontal line at 58 °C; and 1 mark for a downward asymptotic curve of correct curvature to 23 °C.

Variant_2.question_text: On your graph, mark the point H, halfway between the temperature of the wax at time t = 0 and the melting point.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for marking point H halfway along an intermediate horizontal line on the graph.

Variant_2.question_text: The mass of wax is 45 g and its specific latent heat of fusion is 220 J/g. Calculate the quantity of heat energy needed to melt the wax at its melting point.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for using the formula Q = mL or 45 × 220; and 1 mark for the answer: 9900 J.

Variant_1.question_text: Take measurements to determine average values for the length l, the width w and the height h of the wooden block. l = ... w = ... h = ...
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for providing sensible values for the length l, width w, and height h, with repeats seen, at least one measurement to the nearest millimetre or better, and a unit seen on one measurement in parts (a), (b), or (c).

Variant_1.question_text: Set up the apparatus as shown in Fig. 1.2. Clamp the 30 cm ruler vertically with the 30.0 cm end of the ruler in contact with the bench. Adjust the boss holding the spring so that the lower end of the spring is between the 5 cm and 10 cm marks on the ruler. (i) Record the scale reading S1 on the ruler at the lower end of the spring. S1 = ... (ii) Suspend the 200 g mass from the spring and record the new scale reading S2 at the lower end of the spring. S2 = ... (iii) Calculate the extension e of the spring using e = S2 – S1. e = ...
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for S1 and S2 measured with at least one measurement to the nearest millimetre or better and the extension e in the range 5.0 cm to 10.0 cm with a unit seen somewhere.

Variant_1.question_text: Remove the 200 g mass and suspend the block of wood from the spring. Record the new scale reading S3 at the lower end of the spring. Hence determine the new extension eN of the spring. S3 = ... eN = ...
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the new extension eN being greater than the previous extension e.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the mass M of the block of wood using the equation M = 200 × eN / e gram. M = ...
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct substitution into the equation for M and a value for M between 130 g and 170 g.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the density ρ of the wood from which the block is made using the equation ρ = M / (l × w × h). ρ = ...
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the density ρ in the range 0.45 g/cm³ to 0.95 g/cm³, with a unit, and expressed with 2 or 3 significant figures.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 2.2, (i) draw a normal to the line XY at the point M, above and below the line XY, (ii) draw a line from M towards the top left of the page at an angle of 60° to the normal. Label this line L. (iii) Extend this line from M to the right-hand edge of the page. Label this line L′.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a correct normal drawn and the angle of incidence being exactly 60°.

Variant_1.question_text: (i) On Fig. 2.2, draw the outline of the block. (ii) Position the illuminated slit so that a ray of light passes along the line L from the top left of the page towards M. (iii) On Fig. 2.2, mark and label two points P1 and P2 on the ray that leaves the block. You should choose the position of these two points so that the position of the ray can be accurately drawn.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for P1 and P2 labelled with one point within 2 cm of the block and the other beyond the side edge of the block or just above the Fig. 2.2 label.

Variant_1.question_text: (i) Remove the block. Draw a line through P1 and P2 to touch the outline of the block at a point. Label this point N. (ii) Draw a straight line from M to N. (iii) Measure the length l of the line MN. l = ... (iv) Measure the perpendicular distance d between the line L′ and the line through P1 and P2. d = ... (v) Calculate d / l. d / l = ...
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the correct construction inside the block and line L extended as L′. Award 1 mark for l and d measured correctly from approximately parallel lines with at least one measurement to the nearest millimetre and with a unit seen on one of the quantities. Award 1 mark for d / l in the range 0.40 to 0.45.

Variant_1.question_text: Position the block A so that it touches the stop at the top of the ramp, as shown in Fig. 3.1. Add all the masses to the mass hanger to ensure that A remains in contact with the stop. Measure the vertical height h and the distance s along the ramp, as shown in Fig. 3.1. Give your answers in metres. h = ... m s = ... m
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for h in the range 0.09 m to 0.18 m and s in the range 0.700 m to 0.900 m with at least one measurement to the nearest 0.001 m or better. Do not accept answers in cm unless m is crossed out and replaced by cm.

Variant_1.question_text: Remove all masses from the mass hanger. Place A at the bottom of the ramp as shown in Fig. 3.2. (i) Place sufficient slotted masses on the mass hanger to ensure that, when released, A accelerates up the ramp, taking a time of between 1 s and 2 s to reach the stop. Record the mass m and the average time t for the motion.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the total mass m approximately 0.2 kg and between 0.05 kg and 0.300 kg. Do not accept answers in g unless kg is crossed out and replaced by g. Award 1 mark for t repeated with correct average and in the range 0.5 s to 2.5 s.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate (i) the acceleration a using a = 2s / t² m/s², (ii) the component of the weight of A down the ramp, (iii) the frictional force F using F = m a - (m + M) g sin θ, (iv) the frictional force F using the standard formula.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct substitutions into the equations in (i) to (iv). Award 1 mark for correct substitution giving a positive F in the range 0.4 N to 1.2 N with a unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Using two pieces of adhesive tape, stick the wire to the bench and ensure that it is straight. Measure the length L of the wire. L =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring L in the range 98.0 cm to 100.0 cm to the nearest mm or better, with a consistent unit (such as cm) seen for L, x, or d.

Variant_2.question_text: Count the number N of turns of wire on the rod. N =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for counting N in the range 14 to 18 turns, measured to the nearest mm or better for related measurements, with a consistent unit for x, L, or d.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure the length x of the coil as shown in Fig. 1.1. x =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring x in the range 0.4 cm to 2.5 cm to the nearest mm or better, with a consistent unit for x, L, or d.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the diameter d of the wire using d = x / N. d =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct substitution in the formula d = x / N, using values with consistent units for d, x, or L.

Variant_2.question_text: Using the top-pan balance, measure the mass m of the coil of wire. m =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring m in the range 0.5 g to 16 g, considering correct substitution for density in the next part.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the density ρ of the material of the wire using the equation ρ = 4m / (π d² L). ρ =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct substitution in the equation ρ = 4m / (π d² L), leading to a value in the range 2.0 to 10.0 g/cm³ to 1 to 3 significant figures with the unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Determine, as accurately as possible, the average separation s of the lines on Fig. 2.1. s =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using all five gaps or five single measurements averaged, leading to a value for s in the range 0.85 cm to 0.95 cm with a unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe any difference between the image that you see through the lens and the object.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the image is magnified, bigger, or larger.

Variant_2.question_text: State what happens to the magnification of the image and describe how your observations show this.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the magnification increases or gets bigger as x (the height) increases, with a comparison such as the image is magnified more as the lens is raised. If neither mark is scored, award 1 mark for stating that the image gets blurred and becomes diminished.

Variant_2.question_text: When the separation of the lines seen through the lens appears to be double the separation of the lines on the paper, tighten the boss and measure x. x =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for an accurate value of x in the range 5.0 cm to 13.0 cm with a unit, measured to the nearest cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how to obtain a more accurate value for x and determine this accurate value.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for either describing repeat measurements shown with correct average, or explaining how x was measured accurately, such as using a set square to ensure the rule is vertical between the bench and rule or at eye level when recording the value. Then, provide the accurate value of x.

Variant_2.question_text: The Supervisor has set up the apparatus as shown in Fig. 3.1. Move the mass hanger and measure the distance y that the mass hanger falls before block A starts to move.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring y in the range 0.700 m to 0.900 m to the nearest 0.001 m, ensuring the unit is m (do not accept answers in cm unless m is crossed out and replaced by cm).

Variant_2.question_text: Add slotted masses to the mass hanger until block A moves with constant speed across the bench. Record the total mass m on the hanger.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording m in the range 0.050 kg to 0.200 kg (do not accept answers in g unless kg is crossed out and replaced by g).

Variant_2.question_text: Use the stopwatch to measure the time t for block A to travel a distance of 1.00 m. Repeat the measurement at least three times and record your results.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for finding t from repeated measurements, averaged correctly with the unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Use your answers to calculate the frictional force F using the equation F = m_total g - (m_block g).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct substitution with EP > EK, giving F in the range 0.4 N to 1.2 N with the unit, where m_total includes M in the range 0.15 kg to 0.25 kg.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure the p.d. V0 across the terminals of the power supply. V0 =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring V0 in the range 1.0 V to 2.2 V to 0.1 V or better with the unit.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure the length L of the wire between the crocodile clips. L =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring L in the range 0.99 m to 1.01 m and calculating K correctly.

Variant_2.question_text: Move one crocodile clip along the wire to change the length to l and measure the p.d. V across the terminals. V =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring V in the range 0.7 V to 1.6 V to 0.1 V or better, with V less than V0 unless an incorrect V0 is obtained.

Variant_2.question_text: Record your results in a table with columns for l, V, 1/l, and V.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for a table with columns for V, l, 1/V, and l1 with units; 1 mark for correct calculation of 1/V and l1; 1 mark for at least five points with the correct trend (V increases as l increases, not including values of l less than 0.300 m); 1 mark for a range of at least 0.500 m used.

Variant_2.question_text: Plot a graph of V on the y-axis against 1/l on the x-axis.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for axes labelled with units and correct orientation; 1 mark for a suitable scale not based on 3, 6, 7 etc., with plotted data and origin occupying at least 12 cm vertically and 8 cm horizontally; 1 mark for two points plotted correctly within 1/2 small square; 1 mark for the best fit fine straight line through points or crosses.

Variant_2.question_text: Determine the gradient of the graph.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for drawing a straight line or tangent on the graph using values from it; 1 mark for using a triangle that uses more than half the drawn line; 1 mark for correct reading of sides of the triangle from a sensible scale.

Variant_2.question_text: Use your gradient to calculate the value of Rx.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct substitution including R in the range 5.0 Ω to 15.0 Ω; 1 mark for correct calculation giving Rx in the range 1.0 Ω to 8.0 Ω with unit and 2 or 3 significant figures.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the student checks that the metre rule is horizontal. You may draw on Fig. 1.1 if you wish.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following acceptable answers: Measures the height of both ends of the metre rule from the bench and ensures they are the same height; Uses a spirit level to check if the metre rule is horizontal; Aligns the metre rule with a horizontal object such as a windowsill; (If on diagram) Uses a ruler and set-square to measure the height at two places on the metre rule; Checks that the strings are perpendicular to the metre rule using a set square.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1, mark and label the length x.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing at least one horizontal line between pendulum A and pendulum B and accurately labeling it as x.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the student can measure x accurately.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following acceptable answers: Measure from the bench or support to the same point on each bob; Ensure the pendulum is not moving while measuring; Explain how to avoid parallax error, such as viewing the measurement from directly in line with the scale.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.3, plot the graph of N on the y-axis against x / cm on the x-axis. Start your axes from the origin. Draw the smooth curve of best fit.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for axes labeled with quantity and unit (N on y-axis and x / cm on x-axis); 1 mark for linear scales; 1 mark for points plotted accurately; 1 mark for drawing the smooth curve of best fit.

Variant_1.question_text: Give one reason why it is helpful to have the strings close together.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Observe both pendulums together or observe them simultaneously.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest one problem this may cause.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following acceptable answers: The strings may tangle; The bobs may collide.

Variant_1.question_text: The length LA of pendulum A is 85.0 cm. The length LB of pendulum B is 75.0 cm. Use your graph in Fig. 1.3 to obtain a value for N.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a value of 15 ± 1, based on the graph (error carried forward if applicable).

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the value for N when LA is 85.0 cm and LB is 75.0 cm. Give your answer to two significant figures.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for calculating 15.484 (or equivalent working); 1 mark for giving the answer as 15 to two significant figures.

Variant_1.question_text: Comment on whether your two values for N are in agreement.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: If both values are the same, state 'Yes, they are the same'; If values are similar (within ±10%), state 'Yes, they are close enough or within experimental error'; If values differ by more than 10%, state 'No, the difference is outside experimental error or they are too far apart'.

Variant_1.question_text: State the name of a suitable instrument for measuring the heights.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Metre rule.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain what is meant by parallax error.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: An error in taking a measurement due to the position of the observer not being directly in line with the scale.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the student can avoid parallax error when measuring the height of a stool.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: View the measurement from level with the top of the stool, ensuring the eye is directly in line with the scale.

Variant_1.question_text: One of the results is very different from the other four. Suggest how the student used the measuring instrument incorrectly to obtain this result.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The metre rule was used upside down or not aligned properly.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the average value of the height of a stool. Give your answer to 3 significant figures.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks as follows: 1 mark for calculating 62.175 or 62.18 or 62.17 or 62.22; 1 mark for giving the final answer as 62.2 cm to 3 significant figures.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the student calibrates a thermometer.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Place the bulb in pure melting ice; Award 1 mark for: Place the bulb above boiling water or in steam.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how the student can use the small plotting compass to plot the shape of the magnetic field on the card.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Placing a dot, mark, or cross at one end of the compass needle. Award 1 mark for: Moving the compass to point to the previous dot and making a new dot. Award 1 mark for: Repeating this process along one line and then joining the dots. Award 1 mark for: Repeating the process with different starting points or drawing more lines.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the plotting compass must be small.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following: The small compass produces more dots or dots that are closer together; it results in smoother lines; the field due to the compass itself is small.

Variant_2.question_text: Apart from the shape, state what else can be deduced about the magnetic field with this apparatus.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following: The direction or strength of the field; which end is north or south, or the poles of the magnet; where the field is stronger.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the student raises the load at a slow constant speed by pulling on the newton meter.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following: To measure a constant force; there is no accelerating force; to give a steady reading; a variation in speed gives a variation in force.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a reason for using a slow constant speed.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following: To have time to read the meter; it is easier to read the meter; it is easier to keep the speed constant.

Variant_2.question_text: State the force F shown by the newton meter.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: 0.45 N (exact answer required, with unit).

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 2.2, mark the position of the eye of the student when taking the reading.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Marking the eye position to avoid parallax, for example, above, below, or on top of the meter looking towards it.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 2.3, write your value of F from (b)(i). On Fig. 2.4, plot the graph of F / N on the y-axis against W / N on the x-axis. Start your axes from the origin. Draw the straight line of best fit.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Axes labelled with quantity and unit. Award 1 mark for: Scales that are linear. Award 1 mark for: Points plotted accurately. Award 1 mark for: Best fit straight line drawn. Award 1 mark for: The value of F when W=0 is between 0.32 N and 0.36 N (error carried forward from graph allowed).

Variant_2.question_text: Use your graph to find the value of F when W = 0.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: The value of F when W=0 is between 0.32 N and 0.36 N (error carried forward from graph).

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a reason why the line of best fit does not pass through the origin.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following: The weight of the lower pulley; friction of the string over the pulleys; because F is not directly proportional to W.

Variant_2.question_text: Determine the gradient of the line. Show your working. Give your answer to 2 significant figures.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Using a large triangle on the graph and attempting the correct calculation. Award 1 mark for: The gradient is between 0.58 and 0.64 (to 2 significant figures, error carried forward from graph allowed).

Variant_2.question_text: State the colours of the bands on the resistors given to the student.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Both first bands are red and both second bands are yellow. Award 1 mark for: The third bands are black for 24 Ω and orange for 24 kΩ, in the correct order.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe in words how the three resistors are combined to give the smallest possible resistance.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Connecting the three resistors in parallel.

Variant_2.question_text: In the three boxes of Fig. 3.3, draw three other possible combinations of all three resistors connected between points A and B.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Drawing any two correct combinations of the three resistors. Award 1 mark for: Drawing all three correct combinations.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 3.3, mark with the letter E the combination that has this resistance.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Marking the letter E on the correct combination that has a resistance of 36 Ω.
